headline: Tech Against Terrorism issues call for stronger anti-terrorist website enforcement; cites UN Security Council recognition for Somalia work
summary: In early January 2025, Tech Against Terrorism published analysis calling for an international framework to stop terrorist-operated websites, citing that al-Shabaab is Africa's most prolific creator of terrorist online content. Tech Against Terrorism's work was recognized in UN Security Council Resolution 2713, which encouraged its support of Somalia's government in confronting al-Shabaab's online presence. The organization noted that in any given week, 20-25% of all terrorist content found on the internet is likely created by al-Shabaab. The Somali government's efforts to take down al-Shabaab content served as a benchmark example, though the Telegram/TikTok ban (August 2023) remained poorly enforced.
online_dynamics: Tech Against Terrorism's TCAP (Terrorist Content Analytics Platform) was deployed to alert platforms to al-Shabaab content. Despite takedowns, al-Shabaab rebuilt accounts within hours using parallel networks. The 2023 Telegram/TikTok ban in Somalia had minimal impact on AS operations but was used by the group to generate government censorship narratives. VPN use surged following the ban.

sources.publisher: Tech Against Terrorism - The Urgent Need to Stop Terrorist-Operated Websites
CTC West Point - The Online Frontline: Decoding al-Shabaab's Social Media Strategy

headline: Puntland launches major Operation Hilaac against ISIS in Cal Miskaad Mountains
summary: Puntland Security Forces launched an intensified campaign in January 2025 to block all escape routes from ISIS militants hiding in the Al Miskaad mountain range, capturing foreign fighters from Ethiopia, Yemen, and Tanzania and destroying dozens of bases. ISIS reacted with drone strikes on Puntland forces — the first recorded use of explosive-laden drones by ISS — and published denial propaganda in its weekly al-Naba newspaper mocking 'imaginary victories.' IS-Somalia's Manjaniq Media and Al Hijraytain Radio (broadcasting in Somali, Oromo, and Amharic) simultaneously ramped up online recruitment and fundraising via Telegram, targeting Ethiopian and Kenyan Muslims.
online_dynamics: ISS Manjaniq Media launched targeted Telegram propaganda denigrating Puntland and US/UAE forces. Ethiopian and Somali-speaking IS recruiters operated separate Telegram channels for vetted recruits and fundraising. Advisories circulated warning against using Telegram bots to evade detection and to use alternate encrypted apps like Zangi messenger.

sources.publisher: Wikipedia - Islamic State insurgency in Puntland
GNET Research - IS Wilayah Somalia Digital Propaganda
Ujasusi Blog - Tanzanian ISIS Fighters Captured

headline: Government critics disappeared after AI-generated political satire; Gideon Kibet among released abductees
summary: Cartoonist Gideon Kibet ('Bull') and several other critics were abducted after sharing AI-generated images criticizing President Ruto on social media in December 2024; Kibet was released on January 9, 2025, ten days after President Ruto pledged to halt abductions of opponents. CNN reporting confirmed at least 82 government critics had gone missing since June 2024, with some found dead showing signs of torture. The abductions created a severe chilling effect on online expression, particularly targeting those who shared AI-generated satirical imagery of the president.
online_dynamics: AI-generated images of President Ruto in a coffin posted on social media triggered abductions of creators. State surveillance of social media accounts enabled targeted kidnappings. Survivors were warned not to speak to media.

region: Juba (Central Equatoria); Aweil (Northern Bahr el Ghazal)
headline: Anti-Sudanese riots erupt in Juba and Aweil after reports of killings in Sudan
summary: Videos circulating on Facebook and TikTok of South Sudanese civilians allegedly killed by the Sudanese Armed Forces and allied militias in Wad Madani (El Gezira state) triggered retaliatory mob violence in Juba and Aweil. Armed youth looted and burned Sudanese-owned businesses; at least nine people were killed and seven injured. The riots represent the first documented violent spillover of Sudan's civil war into South Sudan, driven by viral social-media content.
online_dynamics: Graphic videos of alleged killings of South Sudanese in Sudan spread virally on Facebook and TikTok, generating calls for retaliation. WhatsApp groups shared location information of Sudanese-owned businesses. Content framed the attacks in Sudan as collective Dinka-versus-Arab or South Sudanese-versus-Sudanese terms, stripping individual context and amplifying collective blame.

headline: BBC report: 'Somalilanders are smitten with Trump' — Project 2025 Somaliland recognition plans spark Somali government alarm
summary: A January 2025 BBC report documented strong Somaliland pro-recognition sentiment ahead of Trump's inauguration, as Project 2025 explicitly called for US recognition of Somaliland to counter Chinese influence in Djibouti. Somalia's government publicly warned that 'any step towards acknowledging Somaliland's independence would violate Somalia's sovereignty and destabilize the region.' Somalia's ambassador to the US stated recognition would 'only embolden extremists and threaten the stability of the entire Horn of Africa.' This politicized discourse created an online environment ripe for disinformation, with competing narratives about recognition implications dominating Somali social media months before Israel's actual recognition in December 2025.
online_dynamics: Pro-Somaliland and pro-Somalia unity accounts engaged in fierce social media battles. Conspiracy theories about US-Israel-Somaliland 'plot against Somalia' began circulating in early 2025, long before Israel's December recognition. Al-Shabaab opportunistically used Somaliland recognition debates to reinforce anti-government narratives about FGS inability to protect Somali sovereignty.

sources.publisher: BBC - Why Somalilanders are smitten with Donald Trump
Happy Medium - Implications of U.S. Recognition of Somaliland

headline: #43against1 campaign pits rest of Kenya against Kikuyu community online
summary: Coordinated hashtag campaign #43against1 emerged on X between January 20–25, 2025, framing Kikuyu voters as complicit in their community's alleged political capture under President Ruto. Nation Africa's year-end analysis identified this as one of the clearest early signals of 2027 ethnic mobilization campaigns. The narrative reactivated longstanding Luo-Kikuyu fault lines and was amplified through cross-platform influence operations.
online_dynamics: Coordinated network repurposed pro-government and anti-government assets to attack different ethnic political targets. Hashtag trended nationally, reaching top Kenyan trends on X.

headline: NCA orders blanket 30-day ban on social media platforms citing violence-related content
summary: The National Communications Authority (NCA) issued orders to all Internet Service Providers on 22 January 2025 to block TikTok and Facebook for a minimum of 30 days—extendable to 90—citing the spread of graphic content related to killings of South Sudanese in Sudan. Following immediate outcry from civil society, the Internet Society South Sudan Chapter, and Amnesty International, authorities reversed the ban within six days on 27 January. The shutdown cost the economy an estimated USD 8.8 million during the six-day period.
online_dynamics: The ban was itself announced through state media and social media, prompting a counter-campaign by civil society on WhatsApp and X (Twitter) using arguments the Internet Society later incorporated into its formal statement. The shutdown demonstrated the government's willingness to use blanket platform restriction as a tool for content control. It also highlighted diaspora amplification: South Sudanese abroad continued sharing restricted content via VPNs.

headline: Trump's first Somalia airstrike kills senior ISIS-Somalia commander and 46 fighters
summary: At President Trump's direction, AFRICOM conducted its first airstrike of his second term on February 1, targeting ISIS-Somalia operatives in the Golis Mountains near Bossaso. Ten strikes targeted cave systems and killed 46 fighters including the deputy to ISIS-Somalia leader Abdul Qadir Mumin, the head of the group's training, and a key recruiter/financier. Secretary of Defense Hegseth personally confirmed the strike, framing it as part of a broader counterterrorism doctrine. This marked the start of a dramatic escalation in US airstrikes that would eventually reach 109+ in 2025 and 26 in January 2026 alone.
online_dynamics: Trump and Hegseth publicized the strike on social media and via press release as a strong national security message. ISIS-Somalia networks on Telegram quickly reframed it as foreign aggression on Muslim lands. ISS propaganda subsequently intensified recruitment messaging leveraging anti-US grievance narratives.

sources.publisher: DefenceWeb - AFRICOM first Trump-directed airstrike on ISIS-Somalia

headline: Senior ISIS-Somalia commander Abdirahman Shirwac surrenders to Puntland forces
summary: Abdirahman Shirwac Aw-Saciid, head of ISIS-Somalia's assassination squad (alias 'Laahoor'), surrendered to authorities in the Cal Miskaad mountains after ongoing US airstrikes and Puntland ground operations. His capture was publicized widely by Puntland authorities as evidence of operational success. ISIS online networks attempted to minimize the significance of the surrender while continuing to publish denial propaganda; ISS media continued to portray surrenders as coercion or fabrication to maintain recruitment appeal among diaspora audiences.
online_dynamics: Puntland authorities publicized the capture through state media and social media channels. ISS-affiliated Telegram channels quickly disputed the narrative, claiming the surrender was fabricated or coerced, to prevent demoralization among online supporters and would-be recruits.

sources.publisher: Al Jazeera - Senior ISIL Somalia commander captured in Puntland

headline: Kenya government ramps up crackdown on AI-generated political content; fear stalks critics
summary: Deutsche Welle documented Kenya's escalating crackdown on AI-generated social media content, with individuals including cartoonist Gideon Kibet and student Billy Mwangi reportedly abducted after sharing AI-generated images depicting President Ruto in a coffin. The Kenya National Human Rights Commission had reported 82 abduction cases since June 2024, with at least 29 people still missing. The crackdown triggered a national debate about free speech versus government regulation of AI-generated content.
online_dynamics: AI-generated satirical imagery deployed as protest tool; state surveillance of social media used to identify and abduct creators. Chilling effect documented through self-censorship among digital activists.

headline: CIPESA report documents hundreds of hate speech incidents across South Sudan social media platforms
summary: A report published by CIPESA (Collaboration on International ICT Policy for East and Southern Africa) found hundreds of documented incidents of hate speech, misinformation, and disinformation across Facebook, WhatsApp, X (Twitter), and TikTok in South Sudan, based on six months of monitoring by DefyHateNow (Digital Rights Frontlines). The report highlighted algorithmic amplification of divisive content, weak regulatory frameworks, and the role of political figures in stoking ethnic tensions online. The findings provided a pre-crisis baseline for the escalation that followed the Nasir clashes in March.
online_dynamics: The report identified recurring narrative patterns around Dinka-Nuer ethnic framing, collective punishment language, and dehumanizing content. It noted that platform algorithms promoted engagement-driving divisive content. Civil society used the report to advocate for stronger regulation, while government officials cited it to justify restrictive legislation.

headline: SSPDF attacks civilians in Nasir marketplace, triggers armed confrontations and airstrikes
summary: Fighting began on 14 February 2025 when the SSPDF attacked civilians in a marketplace in Nasir, leading to armed confrontations with local White Army-aligned youth that displaced thousands. By 25 February, the SSPDF launched airstrikes on SPLA-IO positions in response to what it claimed was a large-scale offensive, with strikes also hitting civilian areas. The National Salvation Front (NAS) issued a statement on 28 February warning of possible war crimes and genocide, accusing the government of orchestrating communal violence by pitting ethnic groups against each other.
online_dynamics: NAS and SPLA-IO released statements online accusing the government of ethnic targeting; government information ministry denied targeting civilians. The narrative of government aggression vs. legitimate community self-defense spread on diaspora Facebook pages and WhatsApp channels, laying groundwork for the hate-speech cascade that accompanied the March escalation.

headline: Telegram removes ISIS-Somalia's Iclaam propaganda channel amid content crackdown
summary: Telegram removed 'Iclaam,' a key ISIS-Somalia propaganda channel that broadcast battlefield reports, ideological messaging, and recruitment content particularly targeting Puntland-region audiences. Despite the removal, ISS maintained presence through Taageere Radio Hijratayn on Facebook, continuing to broadcast content about clashes with Puntland forces. The takedown came during an intensified period of counter-terrorism operations and demonstrated ongoing challenges in fully severing extremist online networks when ISIS moved to backup channels and encrypted alternatives.
online_dynamics: Following removal of the Iclaam channel, ISS supporters quickly migrated to backup channels and alternative platforms. Puntland courts had previously sentenced diaspora members for online endorsement of ISIS content, creating chilling effect on legitimate discourse about IS activities in Puntland.

sources.publisher: Hiiraan Online - Telegram shuts down ISIS propaganda channel

headline: Al-Shabaab launches Operation Ramadan (2025 Shabelle Offensive), most ambitious push in years
summary: On February 20, al-Shabaab launched 'Operation Ramadan' (Hawlgalka Ramadaanka), a massive coordinated offensive targeting Somali military positions in Middle Shabelle, Lower Shabelle, and Hiiraan regions, temporarily capturing dozens of towns including approaching Mogadishu's outskirts for the first time in 10 years. The group used VBIEDs, mortars, and coordinated ground assaults, killing 60-100 Somali soldiers in early days. Al-Kataib Media Foundation rapidly released photo and video documentation of captured towns and military equipment, framing the offensive as religiously mandated during Ramadan. The offensive reversed years of government gains from the 2022 counter-offensive and represented the most significant territorial advance since 2019.
online_dynamics: Al-Kataib released images and videos of captured villages within hours of operations, creating a real-time propaganda campaign showing militants patrolling towns and claiming military equipment. Shahada News Agency published claims of 150+ government casualties on March 15, widely shared on Arabic-language social media. Al-Shabaab clan-affiliated militias (Tunni clan donating camels, Dabarre clan donating goats to fighters) amplified narratives of popular support across social media.

sources.publisher: Security Council Report - Somalia April 2025 Forecast
War on the Rocks - Flailing State: The Resurgence of al-Shabaab

headline: White Army overruns SSPDF base at Nasir; UNMISS helicopters attacked; online hate speech cascade erupts
summary: On 3 March 2025, the White Army overran the SSPDF military base in Nasir (Upper Nile State) following the killing of a White Army member by government forces, capturing the barracks by 4 March. On 7 March, UNMISS helicopters came under fire while evacuating stranded SSPDF personnel, killing UN crew member and SSPDF General David Majur Dak along with over 27 soldiers. The attack triggered an immediate online cascade: Facebook pages and WhatsApp channels erupted with ethnic hate speech targeting Nuer communities, and politicians made inflammatory statements amplified across platforms.
online_dynamics: The attack generated an immediate online hate-speech cascade. Facebook posts inciting collective violence against Nuer communities spread within hours. WhatsApp broadcast channels were used to mobilize youth. Diaspora accounts amplified content internationally. The government simultaneously used state media (SSBC) to frame the events as SPLA-IO terrorism. Civil society organizations issued a joint statement on 18 March specifically calling out the 'rise of hate speech, misinformation, and incitement to violence' around the Nasir conflict.

headline: NCIC issues cessation notice to Gachagua over ethnic incitement in Meru speech; warns against tribal political rhetoric
summary: The National Cohesion and Integration Commission issued a formal cessation notice to impeached former Deputy President Rigathi Gachagua following a speech in Meru in which he accused President Ruto of planning to remove the Chief Justice ahead of the 2027 elections due to her ethnicity. The NCIC warned Gachagua against continuing ethnic incitement, as his political fallout with Ruto was increasingly framed in tribal terms—Kikuyu versus Kalenjin—threatening national cohesion ahead of the 2027 cycle.
online_dynamics: Gachagua's speeches amplified through YouTube streams and political WhatsApp groups. NCIC action generated significant social media debate about double standards in enforcement.

headline: Ethiopia conducts first airstrikes in Somalia since 2006; al-Shabaab claims civilian casualties
summary: The Ethiopian Air Force conducted multiple airstrikes on al-Shabaab targets in the Middle Shabelle region on March 5 — the first known Ethiopian aerial operation in Somalia since its 2006 intervention against the Islamic Courts Union. On the night of March 15, Ethiopia struck El-Ba'ad and Jilib using barrel bombs filled with fuel, with al-Shabaab claiming the El Ba'ad strike killed a mother and her two children and that the Jilib market strike caused extensive civilian casualties. Al-Shabaab immediately distributed a press statement documenting the alleged civilian casualties, using them for international propaganda to frame Ethiopian intervention as illegal foreign aggression against Muslim civilians.
online_dynamics: Al-Shabaab's media apparatus rapidly published Arabic-language press releases citing civilian deaths from Ethiopian strikes across Somali-language social media and Shahada News Agency. The alleged civilian casualties were amplified by al-Shabaab sympathizers on diaspora Twitter/X and Facebook as evidence of 'Western-backed' foreign aggression, strengthening al-Shabaab's anti-government narrative despite questionable sourcing.

sources.publisher: CLAWS - China's Gamble Infrastructure and Influence in Somalia (cites Shahada News Agency)

headline: NSS and military intelligence arrest SPLM-IO oil minister Puot Kang, general Gabriel Duop Lam, and over 20 others
summary: Following the Nasir clashes, South Sudan's NSS and military intelligence arrested at least 22 SPLM-IO members including Minister of Petroleum Puot Kang Chol and SPLA-IO Acting Chief of Staff Lt. General Gabriel Duop Lam. The arrests were denounced by SPLM-IO as a 'grave violation of the peace deal' and a 'blatant infringement of the Constitution.' The detentions signaled a systematic move to decapitate the opposition leadership under the cover of the security crisis.
online_dynamics: SPLM-IO issued statements on Facebook and distributed via WhatsApp condemning the arrests. International media coverage was rapidly shared in diaspora communities, generating grievance narratives. Government disinformation included claiming detained officials had been involved in planning the Nasir attack, shared through state media.

headline: Uganda deploys special forces to South Sudan in violation of UN arms embargo
summary: Uganda deployed special forces and military equipment to South Sudan at President Kiir's request on 11 March 2025, in violation of the UN Security Council arms embargo in force since 2018. The deployment included armoured vehicles, tanks, and military helicopters stationed primarily east of Juba. Neither Uganda nor South Sudan sought prior exemption from the UN Security Council Sanctions Committee. Machar wrote to the UN, AU, and IGAD on 23 March accusing Uganda of conducting airstrikes on civilians.
online_dynamics: Machar's letter to the UN was released publicly and circulated widely on Facebook and WhatsApp by SPLM-IO supporters, reinforcing a narrative of government-allied external aggression against Nuer communities. Government statements denied the arms embargo violation. The deployment became a key element in international community debates about accountability.

headline: Al-Shabaab lays 24-hour siege on Cairo Hotel in Beledweyne, killing clan elders and military officers
summary: Six al-Shabaab militants launched a suicide car bomb and gun attack on the Cairo Hotel in Beledweyne on March 11, where traditional elders and military officers had gathered to coordinate the government's offensive against al-Shabaab. The 24-hour siege killed at least 21 people — including 2 prominent traditional elders and all 6 attackers — and injured dozens. Al-Shabaab framed the attack as a deliberate strategic strike against 'apostate' coordination structures and celebrated the killing of clan elders as elimination of 'pro-government spies.' The attack demonstrated al-Shabaab's intent to neutralize grass-roots anti-AS mobilization structures.
online_dynamics: Al-Shabaab's Shahada News Agency claimed 20+ deaths within hours of the attack. Al-Kataib reportedly released video documentation. The targeting of traditional elders — key anti-AS mobilization figures — was amplified on diaspora social media as evidence that al-Shabaab could penetrate any soft target, spreading fear and deterring elder cooperation with government forces.

sources.publisher: BBC - Somali forces end deadly 24-hour hotel siege
Reuters - Al Shabaab gunmen attack hotel in central Somalia

region: Nasir, Longechuk, Ulang Counties, Upper Nile State
headline: SSPDF uses incendiary weapons in Nasir, Longechuk, and Ulang; information minister implies civilians can be lawfully targeted
summary: From 16 March onward, SSPDF and UPDF conducted aerial attacks using improvised incendiary weapons in at least four locations across Nasir, Longechuk, and Ulang counties, killing at least 58 people and burning others severely—including children. On 17 March, Information Minister Michael Makuei Lueth ordered civilians to evacuate the 'military zone' and stated 'if you as a civilian happen to be there… there is nothing we can do,' implying civilian targeting was lawful—a statement Human Rights Watch later flagged as a possible incitement to war crimes.
online_dynamics: The information minister's statement was broadcast on state radio and quickly spread to Facebook, where it was interpreted by Nuer diaspora pages as an explicit threat to civilians. Government-aligned accounts framed it as legitimate counter-terrorism. Human rights organizations published documentation that circulated internationally. Mobile networks were disrupted in affected areas, limiting on-the-ground reporting.

headline: Al-Shabaab militants storm Kenyan village in Mandera, deliver sermon before withdrawing
summary: Al-Shabaab fighters raided the Kenyan village of Iresuki in Mandera County, engaging National Police Reservists in a gunfight and killing one officer (Abdikher Ibrahim) before withdrawing. According to Hiiraan reports, the militants delivered a public sermon to residents before leaving, a tactic designed to demonstrate control over the territory and recruit sympathizers. This incident was part of al-Shabaab's broader 2025 Ramadan offensive that simultaneously targeted Somalia.
online_dynamics: Al-Shabaab's media wing released imagery of cross-border raids through Telegram channels. The sermons and raids served a dual propaganda function—demonstrating territorial reach into Kenya.

headline: Civil society issues joint statement condemning hate speech and incitement linked to Nasir conflict
summary: A coalition of South Sudanese civil society organizations published a joint statement calling out the 'rise of hate speech, misinformation, and incitement to violence' specifically tied to the Nasir conflict. The statement documented the online cascade pattern: Facebook hate speech → WhatsApp mobilization → government social media shutdown → ethnic polarization. The South Sudan Media Authority issued a separate press statement on 24-25 March warning politicians and social media users against inflammatory speeches.
online_dynamics: The joint statement itself circulated on Facebook and WhatsApp. The Media Authority's press statement was widely shared by state media outlets. Both were countered on social media by partisan accounts dismissing the warnings as government censorship. The pattern revealed a three-layer problem: harmful content, platform amplification, and state response conflating counter-speech with censorship.

headline: Al-Shabaab detonates IED targeting President Hassan Sheikh Mohamud's convoy in Mogadishu
summary: On March 18, al-Shabaab militants planted a roadside bomb near the Ceel-gaabta junction in Xamar-Jajab district, targeting President Hassan Sheikh Mohamud's motorcade as he traveled to the airport. The bomb killed at least 10 people including seven presidential guards and a journalist (Radio Risaala's Mohamed Abukar Dabaashe), and injured 20 others. The president escaped unharmed and continued to the frontlines. Al-Shabaab immediately claimed responsibility via Shahada News Agency and Telegram, framing the attack as an operation to eliminate the 'apostate president' — the first direct assassination attempt on Mohamud since 2014. The attack drew international condemnation from the Arab League, OIC, and UN Secretary-General.
online_dynamics: Al-Shabaab's claim circulated on Telegram within minutes of the attack. The killing of journalist Dabaashe was used by al-Shabaab to spread fear among Somali media. Diaspora social media amplified al-Shabaab's claim that the blast marked a new phase of urban warfare. NISA later announced in September 2025 it had killed the attack mastermind, Mohamed Abdi Dhiblawe Afrax, in Lower Shabelle.

sources.publisher: Wikipedia - Attempted assassination of Hassan Sheikh Mohamud
Africa Defense Forum - Assassination Attempt Highlights al-Shabaab Threat

headline: Al-Shabaab fires mortars at Aden Adde Airport and UN/AU Halane compound in Mogadishu
summary: One day after the presidential assassination attempt, al-Shabaab launched multiple mortar attacks targeting Aden Adde International Airport and the Halane compound, which houses UN headquarters, AU personnel, and multiple foreign embassies. Several shells struck near the Halane perimeter. International airlines including Turkish Airlines and EgyptAir cancelled flights; the attack escalated concerns among the international community about Mogadishu's security. The attack was framed by al-Shabaab as a message directed at foreign actors who 'protect the apostate government.'
online_dynamics: Al-Shabaab's announcement of the mortar strikes on Telegram generated significant media coverage and diaspora social media discussion. The flight cancellations were amplified to signal the growing insecurity around Mogadishu, undermining government narratives of improved security. NISA counter-messaging on state TV and Facebook attempted to reassure the public.

sources.publisher: Wikipedia - Attempted assassination of Hassan Sheikh Mohamud (mortar section)
Al Jazeera - Al-Shabab gunmen lay siege to Somali hotel

headline: ACAPS report: Inter-clan violence doubled in 2024 (168 incidents), displacing 250,000+; expected to worsen in 2025
summary: An ACAPS thematic report (March 19) documented that inter-clan violence in Somalia escalated dramatically in 2024 — from 90 incidents in 2023 to 168 in 2024 — displacing over 250,000 people and killing 600+. Violence centered on Gedo, Lower Juba, and Shabelle regions, expanding south in 2024 into Bay and northern areas like Sanaag and Sool. ACAPS warned clan conflicts would persist into 2025 driven by unresolved land disputes, political rivalries, climate-induced resource scarcity, and 2026 election tensions. Social media and diaspora amplification of clan discourse (including 'Abgaalistan' rhetoric) was identified as an accelerant. Al-Shabaab exploits clan grievances for recruitment.
online_dynamics: Diaspora-based clan advocacy accounts amplify conflict narratives in real time, often before information is verified. 'Abgaalistan' calls (for Abgal clan autonomy/dominance in Mogadishu) circulated on Somali Twitter/X in 2025, inflaming tensions. Humanitarian aid instrumentalization along clan lines was documented. Al-Shabaab recruits through clan grievance exploitation, particularly targeting clan militias that had previously opposed it.

sources.publisher: ReliefWeb/ACAPS - Impact of clan conflicts Somalia (March 2025)

headline: Somali security forces arrest key Al-Shabaab media operative while filming failed attack
summary: Somali security forces arrested Abdullahi Ahmed Mohamed, described as a key operative in al-Shabaab's media efforts, in Waajid, Bakool region, while he was filming a failed attack on a government military base. This arrest highlighted the integration of al-Shabaab's military and media operations: dedicated personnel document attacks in real time for rapid propaganda release. The arrest demonstrated the operational security risks al-Shabaab media operatives take and the Somali government's growing capacity to identify and target them.
online_dynamics: Al-Shabaab typically releases attack footage within minutes-to-hours of operations. The arrest of a dedicated media operative filming an attack documented the synchronized nature of AS military-propaganda operations. NISA publicized the arrest to deter other media operatives and counter al-Shabaab's information warfare.

headline: Al-Shabaab kills six Kenyan police officers in dawn attack on Fafi police reservist camp
summary: Al-Shabaab militants launched a coordinated dawn attack on a police reservist camp in the Fafi area of Garissa County near the Kenya-Somalia border, killing six officers and injuring four. The attack used multiple weapons and was described by Reuters and Al Jazeera as part of the group's ongoing campaign of cross-border assaults. The U.S. Embassy had issued a travel advisory warning about Garissa County just days prior. The incident followed al-Shabaab's Ramadan 2025 offensive and was part of the broader 2025 Shabelle operational escalation.
online_dynamics: Al-Shabaab's media network did not immediately claim responsibility, but later imagery circulated via Telegram. The attack drove significant fear-based messaging in Garissa communities on WhatsApp.

headline: Al-Shabaab sets up checkpoints on Mogadishu-Bal'ad road, ambushes SNA convoy
summary: Al-Shabaab fighters entered the road connecting Mogadishu with Bal'ad on March 23, setting up checkpoints, searching vehicles, and having senior commanders publicly address travelers — the first time in a decade militants appeared on a road so close to the capital. That same day, militants ambushed an SNA convoy in the Hareri Aalde area killing 16 and injuring 12. Al-Kataib media released video footage of the convoy ambush widely distributed on Telegram and Facebook. The checkpoints and public presence were used by al-Shabaab for psychological warfare to demonstrate that the government could not protect citizens even near Mogadishu.
online_dynamics: Al-Kataib video of the convoy ambush was shared across 13+ Telegram channels within minutes. The checkpoint images were amplified on diaspora Facebook and Twitter/X, generating fear narratives about government collapse. The public address by al-Shabaab commanders on a main road near Mogadishu generated significant online commentary questioning government authority.

headline: Riek Machar placed under house arrest; SPLM-IO declares peace agreement collapsed
summary: On 26 March 2025, South Sudan's defense minister and national security chief forcibly entered Machar's residence in Juba, presented an arrest warrant, disarmed his bodyguards, and placed the First Vice President under house arrest. The SPLM-IO immediately declared this 'a blatant infringement of the Constitution and the Revitalized Peace Agreement,' and called the arrests 'politically motivated.' The AU Panel of the Wise, special envoy Raila Odinga, and the AU Delegation were subsequently denied access to Machar.
online_dynamics: SPLM-IO released statements on Facebook and Twitter denouncing the arrest. Reuters's report was shared thousands of times in South Sudanese diaspora groups. Government-aligned accounts on Facebook framed the arrest as a necessary security measure. The event dominated online discourse among South Sudanese globally for weeks and became a reference point for disinformation—with both sides releasing contradictory accounts of events.

headline: Al-Shabaab recaptures Masajid Ali Gaduud, captured by government in 2022
summary: Al-Shabaab fighters retook the strategic town of Masajid Ali Gaduud near Adale after heavy confrontation, reversing a significant gain from the 2022 government offensive. The town's fall came after the group had already captured Al-Shabaab fighters from the Aboorey area, including fighters reportedly of Oromo ethnicity — highlighting the transnational nature of al-Shabaab's force composition. Al-Shabaab used the recapture to illustrate in its propaganda that no government gain was permanent and that the organization's campaign was progressing.
online_dynamics: Al-Shabaab propaganda framed the recapture of previously-lost territory as proof of divine support and government weakness. The presence of Oromo ethnicity fighters in al-Shabaab's ranks was not prominently mentioned in AS propaganda, but security analysts noted it as evidence of al-Shabaab's active transnational recruitment.

headline: Al-Shabaab cancels annual Eid military parade, leaders communicate only via voice messages
summary: Al-Shabaab cancelled its annual Eid al-Fitr military parade — a major propaganda tool used to demonstrate military strength and weapons — citing the impact of ongoing airstrikes. No troops or officials appeared in parade grounds; leaders only participated in Eid celebrations via voice messages rather than public appearances. This marked a significant disruption to al-Shabaab's propaganda machinery and signaled that US, Ethiopian, and Somali airstrikes had succeeded in limiting the group's freedom of movement and public operations. Somali government and international partners amplified this as evidence of military effectiveness.
online_dynamics: Al-Shabaab still released Eid messaging via Telegram and radio but the absence of the traditional military parade was widely noted by Somali journalists and international analysts. NISA and the Somali government circulated news of the parade cancellation as a counter-narrative success, framing it as proof that the government's 'total war' was degrading AS capacity.

sources.publisher: Hiiraan Online - Al-Shabaab cancels Eid military parades as airstrikes intensify
Somali Magazine - Al-Shabaab Cancels Eid Parades Amid Intensified Airstrikes

headline: #ToxicActivists campaign targets journalist Hanifa Adan with Islamophobic disinformation after BBC 'Blood Parliament' documentary
summary: Following journalist Hanifa Adan's BBC interview about the 'Blood Parliament' documentary investigating the June 25, 2024 police shootings of protesters, suspected state-supported trolls launched the #ToxicActivists campaign targeting her. Organized trolls posted Islamophobic imagery and coordinated posts describing her as a 'foreigner,' 'fool,' and 'Somali terrorist' to discredit her and other prominent activists. Amnesty International documented this as part of state-coordinated digital repression, with the campaign exploiting ethnic and religious tensions to silence accountability journalism.
online_dynamics: Mass-posting of identical messages to hijack X's algorithm. Islamophobic imagery amplified via coordinated retweet chains. Campaign designed to discredit activist-journalists by framing them as foreign agents.

headline: Fulcrum Analytics and SETAV assess Al-Shabaab's evolved 2025 media strategy: pseudo-civilian profiles, female accounts, AI potential
summary: Multiple April 2025 analyses documented al-Shabaab's evolved media strategy. Fulcrum Analytics reported the group exploits 'pseudo-civilian profiles' and female accounts to evade detection while distributing content. SETAV documented the shift from cassette sermons (2006-2014) to digital propaganda leveraging AI-generated content for improved reach. The 2025 offensive demonstrated integration of propaganda with military operations — Al-Kataib documented territorial gains in near-real-time. Analysts noted al-Shabaab was exploring large language models for propaganda production. Tech Against Terrorism noted the group produces 20-25% of all online terrorist content weekly.
online_dynamics: Al-Shabaab deployed pseudo-civilian accounts and female personas to bypass platform content moderation. The group's coordinated network of 250+ Telegram channels and 190+ Facebook accounts operated with near-military discipline. Accounts that were taken down were rebuilt within hours using pre-established parallel networks. Al-Shabaab specifically used Gaza conflict content to expand reach beyond Somalia.

sources.publisher: Fulcrum Analytics - Hearts and Minds: Al Shabaab's Propaganda Machine
SETAV - Al-Shabab's Evolving Media Strategy: Narratives, Tools, and Impact 2006-2025
Africa Defense Forum - Defeating Terrorist Content Online Requires Unified Approach

headline: Al-Shabaab fires mortars at Mogadishu airport, disrupting international flights
summary: Al-Shabaab militants fired multiple mortar rounds at Aden Adde International Airport on April 6, disrupting international flights. A Turkish Airlines plane was rerouted to Djibouti and EgyptAir cancelled its flight. The Halane camp (UN, AU, diplomatic missions) was also targeted. The attack came weeks after the assassination attempt on the president and escalated a pattern of strategic targeting of Mogadishu infrastructure. No casualties were reported, but the attack reinforced perceptions of insecurity around the capital.
online_dynamics: Flight cancellations were widely reported on Somali social media and created significant public anxiety. Al-Shabaab's claim was distributed on Telegram, generating diaspora amplification of the insecurity narrative. Government spokespeople attempted to downplay casualties, while al-Shabaab amplified disruption to international aviation as a key propaganda win.

sources.publisher: The Defense Post - Al-Shabaab Launches Mortar Attacks Near Somalia's Main Airport
Arab News - Al-Shabab launches mortar attacks near Somalia's main airport

headline: MSF hospital in Ulang violently looted; 150,000 people lose access to healthcare
summary: On 14 April 2025, dozens of armed men stormed the MSF hospital in Ulang, Upper Nile State, threatened staff, looted nine months' worth of medical supplies worth over USD 150,000, and completely destroyed the facility's infrastructure. MSF was forced to permanently close the hospital and suspend support to 13 primary health facilities, leaving 150,000 people without secondary health care. This was the second attack on MSF in the area within three months, following a January 2025 attack on MSF boats near Nasir.
online_dynamics: MSF publicized the attack on social media and issued press releases that were widely shared. The attacks were used by different factions online to blame each other—government-aligned accounts attributed the looting to White Army, while opposition accounts blamed SSPDF-aligned militias. The denial of attribution contributed to disinformation about accountability.

region: Lower Shabelle / Lower Juba / Middle Juba
headline: US-Gulf TFTC sanctions 15 Al-Shabaab financiers, IED facilitators, and regional commanders
summary: The seven-member US-Gulf Terrorist Financing Targeting Center (TFTC) jointly sanctioned 15 Al-Shabaab leaders, operatives, and financial facilitators operating primarily in Lower Shabelle, Lower Juba, and Middle Juba. Those sanctioned were accused of illegal taxation of civilians, kidnappings, livestock seizures, Hisbah enforcement, facilitating IED manufacture, and running detention centers. The action marked the eighth TFTC joint designation and aimed to sever al-Shabaab's access to over $100 million per year in extortion revenue. Al-Shabaab propaganda downplayed the sanctions as ineffective 'paper measures.'
actors: Saudi Arabia, UAE, Qatar, Oman, Kuwait, Bahrain (TFTC)
online_dynamics: The Treasury designation generated extensive international media coverage framing al-Shabaab as a financial threat. Al-Shabaab's media networks minimized the sanctions, questioning their real-world impact. The revelation that individual AS commanders collected millions through livestock seizures and market taxes generated Somali social media discussion on AS's shadow governance.

sources.publisher: US Department of the Treasury - TFTC Designates Al-Shabaab Operatives
Bloomberg - US, Gulf States Sanction Somali Terror Group Facilitators
Hiiraan Online - U.S., Gulf states sanction 15 al-Shabaab figures

headline: US-Somali airstrike destroys al-Shabaab ship carrying sophisticated weapons near Baraawe
summary: On April 16-17, 2025, US and Somali security forces conducted an airstrike targeting unmarked vessels carrying sophisticated weapons belonging to al-Shabaab near Baraawe, Lower Shabelle. This operation was directly linked to documented Houthi-al-Shabaab weapons transfers: the UN had found al-Shabaab received weapons from Yemen (June-September 2024), and the group had operatives returning from AQAP drone training. The interception of a weapons shipment near Baraawe demonstrated the Red Sea axis arms trafficking route was actively being used to supply al-Shabaab's 2025 offensive.
online_dynamics: The interception of weapons shipments received limited public online amplification compared to kinetic attacks, but security analysts highlighted it as evidence of the Houthi-AS nexus. Al-Shabaab networks did not claim the vessels publicly. International media coverage focused on the transnational arms trafficking dimension.

sources.publisher: Africa Center for Strategic Studies - Expanding Al Shabaab–Houthi Ties

headline: White Army executes tactical withdrawal; SSPDF peacefully retakes Nasir
summary: With coordination from local community leaders, the White Army conducted a 'tactical withdrawal' from Nasir on 20 April 2025, allowing SSPDF forces to peacefully reoccupy the town. SSPDF spokesperson Lul Ruai Koang stated the army's operations had concluded. Chief of Defense Forces Paul Nang Majok visited Nasir on 21 April to congratulate troops. However, displacement from the conflict had already reached 80,000+ people, with tens of thousands having fled into Ethiopia.
online_dynamics: Government accounts celebrated the 'recapture' on state media and social media. Opposition and diaspora accounts framed it as a military defeat for the White Army after weeks of airstrikes and described ongoing civilian displacement. The conflicting narratives—military success vs. continued humanitarian crisis—illustrate the disinformation environment around the conflict.

headline: Al-Shabaab executes government informant in Janaale after Sharia court conviction
summary: Al-Shabaab's Islamic Sharia court in Janaale District, Lower Shabelle, executed 27-year-old Abdullahi Abdirahman Ali after convicting him of working with the federal government's intelligence service. The execution was publicized on Shahada News Agency and Telegram as a demonstration of al-Shabaab's functioning governance and judicial system. These executions served as a key intimidation mechanism to deter civilian cooperation with NISA and government forces, while simultaneously projecting al-Shabaab's claim to legitimate Islamic governance authority.
online_dynamics: Al-Shabaab publicized the execution through Shahada News Agency in Arabic, framing it under Islamic jurisprudence as the just punishment for 'spying for infidels.' The announcement was distributed on Telegram channels to deter other civilians from cooperating with government intelligence, creating chilling effects on counter-terrorism information gathering.

headline: Al-Shabaab mortar attack rocks Mogadishu Halane base hosting Western diplomats (April 28, 2025)
summary: Explosions struck the Mogadishu Halane compound on April 28, 2025, in a mortar attack targeting the heavily fortified base that houses Western diplomatic missions, UN offices, and the AU mission headquarters. This was the second significant mortar strike on the compound in April (after the April 6 airport attack), establishing a pattern of sustained pressure on the international presence in Mogadishu. The repetitive targeting sought to erode the willingness of international partners to maintain their presence in Somalia.
online_dynamics: The April mortar attacks generated ongoing discussions among the international diplomatic community about security protocols. Al-Shabaab's sustained targeting of the Halane compound was a deliberate information operation — demonstrating to the world that Mogadishu remained under threat despite government counter-narratives about improved security.

sources.publisher: Somali Guardian - Explosions rock Mogadishu base hosting western diplomats

headline: Al-Shabaab kills five police officers in Boni Forest ambush at Rakei area, Lamu County
summary: Al-Shabaab militants ambushed and killed five police officers while injuring eight others at the Rakei area in Boni Forest, Lamu County. This was among the deadliest single incidents targeting Kenya's security forces in Boni Forest, where Operation Amani Boni has been ongoing since 2015. The attack followed a series of IED and ambush operations by the group in the forest region, which al-Shabaab has used as a staging ground for cross-border attacks.
online_dynamics: Al-Shabaab's media network released operational claims via Telegram. The attack received extensive coverage on Citizen TV and other Kenyan media, spreading fear in coastal communities.

headline: SSPDF helicopter gunships bomb MSF hospital and market in Old Fangak; 7 killed, 20 injured
summary: At 4:30 a.m. on 3 May 2025, two SSPDF helicopter gunships bombed the MSF pharmacy at Old Fangak hospital in Jonglei State, burning it to the ground, then fired on the town for 30 minutes. A drone subsequently bombed the Old Fangak market. At least seven people were killed and 20 injured. The hospital was the only secondary health facility for 110,000+ people. MSF had shared GPS coordinates with all conflict parties prior to the attack. The bombing was the most high-profile attack on healthcare infrastructure during the 2025 escalation.
online_dynamics: MSF's condemnation went viral internationally, amplified by human rights organizations on Twitter and Facebook. South Sudanese diaspora used the attack to support narratives of government targeting of Nuer communities. Government denied deliberate targeting. The disinformation gap between official denial and on-the-ground evidence was documented and amplified by international media.

headline: GNET documents ISIS-Somalia Manjaniq Media propaganda on Telegram: transnational recruitment via Amharic/Oromo channels
summary: A May 2025 GNET Research analysis documented ISIS-Somalia's Manjaniq Media on Telegram and the role of Amharic/Oromo-speaking propagandists in facilitating fundraising and recruitment from Ethiopia and Kenya. Two prominent Ethiopian IS recruiters operated separate Telegram channels directing vetted recruits to Ethiopian fighters in Somalia. During Ramadan, crowdfunding campaigns ran in Telegram groups with direct connections to ISS operatives. Al Hijraytain Radio broadcast in Somali, Oromo, and Amharic targeting East African recruits. ISS has an estimated 700-1,500 fighters with 60-70% being foreign nationals.
online_dynamics: Pro-ISS Telegram recruiters advised followers not to follow IS-linked Telegram bots (to avoid detection) and to use non-revealing usernames and profile pictures. Female IS supporters participated in discussion groups facilitating vetting of potential recruits. Ethiopian and Oromo-language content specifically targeted Muslims with grievances about Ethiopian state violence to facilitate hijrah (migration) to ISS-controlled territory in Puntland.

sources.publisher: GNET Research - IS Wilayah Somalia: Digital Propaganda and Strategy Against the Puntland

headline: UN Security Council extends UNMISS mandate; US warns South Sudan 'on brink of broader civil war'
summary: The UN Security Council extended the UNMISS mandate for one year (until April 2026) by a vote of 12-0 with 3 abstentions (China, Russia, Pakistan). The US representative warned South Sudan was 'on the brink of a broader civil war,' citing aerial bombardments, the indiscriminate use of barrel bombs, an attack on a hospital, and the detention of First Vice President Machar. The resolution demanded all parties immediately cease fighting and called on the transitional government to cooperate with UNMISS.
online_dynamics: The UNSC resolution was amplified by diaspora communities and civil society as validation of their concerns. Government accounts on social media downplayed the 'civil war' characterization. The abstentions by China and Russia were highlighted by opposition-aligned accounts as evidence of impunity.

headline: Student activist Billy Mwangi abducted and tortured for posting AI-generated satire of President Ruto
summary: Student activist Billy Mwangi was abducted from outside his barbershop and tortured after a social media account associated with him posted an AI-generated satirical image of President Ruto. Mwangi was held for approximately 15 days before being released without charge. This was documented as one of at least 82 abduction incidents tracked by human rights monitors since June 2024. The case became emblematic of the government's use of intimidation to suppress AI-generated political satire.
online_dynamics: AI-generated image of Ruto circulated widely before account was suspended. Mwangi's abduction reported on social media by rights groups, generating calls for accountability. State surveillance linked to tracking of social media account.

headline: Al-Shabaab suicide bomber targets army recruitment queue at Damaanyo base, killing 20
summary: An Al-Shabaab suicide bomber disguised in military uniform detonated explosives at the entrance of the Damaanyo military base in Mogadishu's Hodan district on May 18, where hundreds of young men were queued to enlist in the Somali National Army. The attack killed at least 20 people (15 recruits and 5 civilians) and injured 30+. Al-Shabaab claimed on Telegram that 100 soldiers were killed — a significantly inflated figure. The attack specifically targeted the pipeline of recruits for the government's anti-AS campaign. Two days later (May 20), al-Shabaab fired 6 mortar rounds at Aden Adde Airport and the Halane compound.
online_dynamics: Al-Shabaab posted claim on Telegram with highly inflated casualty figures (100 killed vs. actual ~20). The attack generated horror and outrage on Somali social media. Al-Shabaab's targeting of young recruits was framed in propaganda as a message to youth: 'Do not serve the apostate government.' The government response emphasized the bravery of those killed, amplifying counter-recruitment messaging on state media.

sources.publisher: Reuters - Mogadishu suicide bomber kills at least 10 at army recruitment drive
AP - Suicide attacker in Somali capital kills 13 at a military recruitment center

headline: Al-Shabaab fires 6 mortar rounds at Halane compound housing UN, AU, and Western embassies
summary: Two days after the Damaanyo base bombing, al-Shabaab fired at least six mortar shells toward the Halane Base Camp in Mogadishu, targeting the compound that hosts United Nations offices, African Union personnel, and Western diplomatic missions. Several rounds landed in and around the Halane perimeter. This attack, following rapidly on the heels of the recruitment center bombing, demonstrated a deliberate escalation campaign designed to signal that no location in Mogadishu was safe from attack.
online_dynamics: The attack on the UN compound was broadcast by Hiiraan Online and international wire services. Al-Shabaab's claim appeared on Telegram, framing the UN as an 'infidel' organization supporting the apostate government. International diplomatic and aid community concern generated discussion about evacuation protocols.

sources.publisher: Hiiraan Online - Al-Shabaab launches mortar attack on Halane Base Camp

headline: Africa Center report confirms Al-Shabaab-Houthi arms and training cooperation since mid-2024
summary: An Africa Center for Strategic Studies report (May 28) and subsequent UN Panel of Experts findings confirmed that Yemen's Houthi rebels and al-Shabaab have engaged in sustained cooperation: the Houthis transfer weapons and provide drone technology training to al-Shabaab, while al-Shabaab facilitates piracy operations and arms trafficking routes from Iran through Somali ports. The UN documented physical meetings in 2024 and weapons shipments from Yemen used in al-Shabaab attacks on AU forces in September and November 2024. Al-Shabaab sent 12+ operatives to Yemen for AQAP training including in drone technology. ISS-Somalia was also found to be experimenting with armed drones.
online_dynamics: Al-Shabaab's Shahada News Agency had been amplifying pro-Houthi and AQAP narratives since October 7, framing Red Sea solidarity as a unified Islamic resistance. The arms cooperation was primarily covert, but al-Shabaab used drone surveillance footage in propaganda videos. UN disclosures generated Western counter-narrative efforts highlighting the Iran-Houthi-AS axis.

sources.publisher: Africa Center for Strategic Studies - Expanding Al Shabaab–Houthi Ties
Africa Defense Forum - U.N. Report Shows Increasing Collaboration of Houthis, al-Shabaab
ISS Africa - Houthis in Somalia: friends with technological benefits?

headline: SSPDF/NSS conduct sweeping arrests of young men and women; UNMISS documents CRSV surge Apr-Jun 2025
summary: From late June 2025, SSPDF and NSS forces conducted sweeping arrests of boys, young men, and women 'under the guise of a crackdown on criminals.' Young women were sexually assaulted during arrests; some young men were forcibly conscripted; others disappeared. UNMISS documented 334 incidents of conflict-related violence in the April-June 2025 quarter affecting 1,518 civilians (635 killed, 676 injured, 133 abducted, 74 subjected to CRSV)—a 43% increase in total victims compared to the same period in 2024.
online_dynamics: Families of detainees used Facebook to post missing persons inquiries and appeals for information. Opposition accounts used the arrest patterns to reinforce ethnic targeting narratives. Government and state media were largely silent on the sweeping arrests. Human rights organizations documented and published findings that circulated online.

headline: Al-Shabaab SVBIED kills 12+ Ugandan AUSSOM peacekeepers in Lower Shabelle
summary: In June 2025, al-Shabaab conducted a suicide VBIED attack targeting a convoy of Ugandan troops serving with AUSSOM in the towns of Sabiid and Anole, killing close to a dozen Ugandan soldiers. This attack preceded a larger second SVBIED attack in July that killed 20 more Ugandan peacekeepers, ultimately triggering Uganda's withdrawal from the area. The June attack demonstrated al-Shabaab's strategic use of VBIEDs to specifically target African Union forces and erode the will of contributing nations to maintain troop deployments.
online_dynamics: Al-Shabaab's media celebrated the attack on Ugandan forces as a demonstration of its anti-foreign-occupation narrative. Claims were amplified on Arabic social media. The attacks generated debate in Uganda about the costs and wisdom of AUSSOM participation, creating political pressure al-Shabaab explicitly sought to exploit through targeted propaganda.

sources.publisher: Security Council Report - Somalia October 2025 Monthly Forecast
The Soufan Center - Al-Shabaab's 2025 Offensive and the Unraveling of Somalia's Security

headline: Puntland captures first Turkish ISIS foreign fighter in Cal Miskaad, claims 98% of mountains reclaimed
summary: Puntland Security Forces captured a Turkish ISIS foreign fighter — the first confirmed Turkish national in IS-Somalia's Cal Miskaad operations — on June 14, 2025, alongside announcing the capture of settlements Sadow, Cadalle, and Raa-raha valley. By June 16, Puntland claimed 98% reclamation of the Cal Miskaad mountain range, with the Fourth Phase of Operation Hilaac launching. The capture of a Turkish fighter demonstrated ISS's global recruitment reach — confirmed fighters included nationals from Ethiopia, Yemen, Morocco, Turkey, Saudi Arabia, Syria, and Tanzania. By August, Puntland forces announced killing 700+ foreign fighters in 8 months.
online_dynamics: The capture of a Turkish national in Somalia generated significant Turkish and Somali media coverage. ISS's Al Hijraytain Radio continued broadcasting recruitment content in Oromo and Amharic targeting Ethiopian audiences. The announcement of 98% reclamation was celebrated on Puntland social media, though ISS continued to hold out in Baalade valley.

sources.publisher: Wikipedia - Islamic State insurgency in Puntland (June 2025 section)
GNET Research - IS Wilayah Somalia Digital Propaganda

headline: Gen-Z anniversary protests: 16 killed, 400+ injured; government deploys state trolls and internet shutdown orders
summary: On the first anniversary of the June 25, 2024 parliament storming, hundreds of thousands protested across 27 counties under hashtags #RutoMustGo, #OccupyStateHouse, and #SiriNiNumbers. Security forces killed at least 16 people and injured over 400 using live ammunition, tear gas, and water cannons. The Communications Authority ordered a halt to all live television and radio coverage, and Telegram was restricted. Interior Minister Murkomen labeled the protests 'terrorism disguised as dissent,' while state-paid troll networks countered protest hashtags in real time with #RutoMustGoOn.
online_dynamics: Protest organized via WhatsApp, Telegram, X, and TikTok. State trolls counter-trended protest hashtags. Live coverage banned on television. Telegram restricted. International embassies (US, UK, Germany, Canada) condemned the crackdown via social media.

headline: July 7, 2025: Kenya's deadliest protest day — 31+ killed, government deploys goons and bans live coverage
summary: July 7, 2025 became the deadliest day of protest in Kenya that year, with at least 31 killed and over 100 injured as police fired live ammunition into crowds in Nairobi's working-class districts. The Kenya National Commission on Human Rights documented 10 fatalities, 29 injuries, 2 abductions, and 37 arrests across 17 counties on July 7 alone. KNCHR observed criminal gangs with weapons collaborating with police to attack protesters. The government's order banning live coverage was later revoked by a court, but the event marked a major escalation of state violence against dissent.
online_dynamics: TikTok livestreams documented real-time violence, bypassing mainstream media blackout. Pro-government networks spread disinformation framing protesters as criminals. #JusticeForGenZ and #RutoMustGo hashtags dominated Kenyan social media. Disinformation included fabricated images of protesters with foreign flags.

headline: AFRICOM conducts airstrike against ISIS-Somalia southeast of Bossaso in July 2025 campaign
summary: U.S. Africa Command conducted an airstrike against ISIS-Somalia on July 6, 2025, southeast of Bossaso in Puntland's northeastern Somalia, as part of an ongoing campaign that had already conducted 30+ strikes against IS positions in the Cal Miskaad range through July. The 2025 AFRICOM pace against ISIS far outstripped that against al-Shabaab (fewer than 24 AS strikes by July), a strategic imbalance that Somali and international analysts criticized as allowing al-Shabaab's momentum in central/southern Somalia to continue unchecked.
online_dynamics: The disparity between US focus on ISIS (30+ strikes) vs al-Shabaab (<24 strikes) by July 2025 was documented by ACLED and became a talking point among Somali political commentators, with al-Shabaab propaganda specifically highlighting it to claim the government was dependent on foreign forces who had their own agenda.

sources.publisher: AFRICOM Press Release - US Forces Conduct Strike Targeting ISIS-Somalia

headline: Al-Shabaab captures Moqokori via SVBIED attacks; claims 47 government/militia killed
summary: Al-Shabaab seized Moqokori in early July 2025 following coordinated SVBIED attacks on government positions, reportedly killing 47 Somali soldiers and Macawiisley militiamen (with 65 wounded) and capturing substantial weapons. The fall of Tardo to al-Shabaab on July 14 followed without resistance. Al-Shabaab immediately set up roadblocks, collected taxes, and began governing Moqokori, with propaganda images showing militants engaging with local residents. By July 24, the group controlled a strategic triangle of Moqokori, Tardo, and Buq-Aqable encircling central Somalia.
online_dynamics: Al-Kataib released propaganda images of militants engaging with Moqokori residents in a deliberate legitimization strategy — a departure from previous forced displacement tactics. Shahada News Agency published detailed casualty claims. The fall of these towns without resistance generated significant online commentary questioning the viability of Macawiisley militias and government forces.

sources.publisher: The Soufan Center - Al-Shabaab's 2025 Offensive and the Unraveling of Somalia's Security
Security Council Report - Somalia October 2025 Monthly Forecast

headline: Shahada News Agency international expansion: new Facebook, Twitter/X, Telegram accounts launched in 2024, active through 2025
summary: Following Shahada News Agency's July 9, 2024 announcement to expand internationally across Facebook, Twitter/X, and Telegram — and continue using encrypted platforms RocketChat and Chirpwire — the agency's expanded presence was fully operational through 2025. By 2025, Shahada was reportedly producing 20-25% of all terrorist content found on the internet weekly, according to Tech Against Terrorism executive Adam Hadley. In August 2024, it reported AQAP aid to Gaza, building Al-Shabaab's pro-Palestinian narrative. Telegram bots were deployed to auto-push attack updates. A Code for Africa investigation linked the agency's registration to a 'Ridwaan Hassan Ali' in Mogadishu, also connected to Al-Kataib channels.
online_dynamics: Shahada News Agency uses a network of 'amplifier' accounts to push content across platforms within minutes of al-Shabaab attacks. Three X accounts (@Gurey1140, @abdisalambahdon) systematically replicated Shahada Chirpwire content to mainstream platforms. The agency deployed Telegram bots for automated updates. Code for Africa linked the same operator ('Ridwaan') to both Shahada News and Al-Kataib's private Telegram channel 'KT foundation,' suggesting coordinated media infrastructure.

sources.publisher: ADDO/African Digital Democracy Observatory - Inside Al-Shabaab's digital propaganda machine
Africa Defense Forum - A Stubborn Insurgency
CTC West Point - The Online Frontline: Decoding al-Shabaab's Social Media Strategy

headline: Deepfakes and AI-generated content deployed by both protesters and state actors during July 2025 protest wave
summary: Analysis by Global Voices Advox and Vellum Kenya's Baraza Media Lab documented the dual deployment of AI during the July 2025 protest wave: protesters used AI to translate and summarize the Finance Bill into Swahili and local languages, while pro-government actors fabricated protest imagery showing foreign flags and deployed deepfake face-swaps to discredit opposition leaders. CNN's Fareed Zakaria's voice and likeness was used repeatedly in AI-generated videos by state-aligned actors, suggesting a trained AI model was being reused. Psychological operations using 'BloodDonation Wednesday' death warnings were deployed to deter protest attendance.
online_dynamics: AI-generated content used by both sides. Protesters used Zello app and ChatGPT for organization. Pro-government actors deployed deepfakes and coordinated psyops. Fear-based narratives including death warnings spread via WhatsApp.

headline: Al-Shabaab IED kills three KDF soldiers and injures six in Boni Forest, Lamu
summary: Three Kenya Defence Forces soldiers were killed and six others injured when their patrol vehicle detonated an IED planted by al-Shabaab in the Bada area of Boni Forest, approximately 2 km from the Kenya-Somalia border. The soldiers were on patrol along the Bada-Sankuri road when the explosion occurred. This incident represented an escalation in al-Shabaab's use of IEDs targeting military vehicles in the Boni Forest region during Kenya's ongoing Operation Amani Boni.
online_dynamics: Operational claim released by al-Shabaab's media wing. NTV Kenya's coast reporter documented the incident. Spread of fear-based messaging in Lamu coastal communities via WhatsApp.

headline: Al-Shabaab SVBIED kills 20 Ugandan AUSSOM soldiers, triggers Ugandan withdrawal from Sabiid/Anole
summary: On July 20, a second SVBIED attack on Ugandan AUSSOM peacekeepers in Sabiid and Anole killed approximately 20 soldiers. Following this attack, Uganda withdrew its forces from forward positions, and both Ugandan and Somali government forces abandoned defensive operations. Al-Shabaab immediately recaptured Sabiid and Anole — key towns 40km southwest of Mogadishu — reversing major 2022 government gains. The withdrawal allowed al-Shabaab to advance to within 50km of Mogadishu, encircling the capital from multiple directions.
online_dynamics: Al-Shabaab's propaganda machine celebrated the Ugandan withdrawal as a major strategic victory and proof that the 'crusader forces' could not withstand Islamic resistance. The withdrawal generated significant social media debate about AUSSOM's reliability and the viability of the government's security strategy. Images of al-Shabaab fighters in Sabiid and Anole were widely shared.

sources.publisher: Security Council Report - Somalia October 2025 Monthly Forecast

headline: Al-Shabaab takes control of Mahaas in Hiiraan — key government hub in central Somalia
summary: Al-Shabaab militants took control of Mahaas in Hiiraan, described as a key government outpost and important hub for operations against the group in central Somalia. The loss of Mahaas came amid a rapid expansion that gave al-Shabaab control of a strategic triangle across Moqokori, Tardo, and Buq-Aqable — and now Mahaas — effectively encircling key government-held areas of Hiran and Middle Shabelle. By late July, NISA claimed an airstrike near Buq-Able killed 7 militants, but such operations had marginal impact on the insurgency's momentum.
online_dynamics: The rapid expansion of al-Shabaab's territorial control through July generated intense online commentary, with Somali opposition politicians using social media to criticize the government's lack of strategic response. Al-Shabaab propaganda framed the territorial gains as proof that its 'total war' against the government was succeeding.

sources.publisher: Security Council Report - Somalia October 2025 Monthly Forecast

headline: Washington Post documents how political operatives recruited youth 'goons' to attack protesters during 2025 protest waves
summary: The Washington Post documented in detail how political operatives recruited young men as 'goons' during the 2025 protest waves—offering payment and equipment to monitor streets and menace demonstrators—while other youth simultaneously risked their lives as protesters. The investigation revealed the fragile social divides being exploited by political networks to suppress civic action, with some goons coming from the same communities as protesters. Interior CS Murkomen later told Parliament that 579 people had been killed in mob justice incidents since January 2025, partly attributed to politicians arming gangs.
online_dynamics: Goon recruitment organized via WhatsApp groups and political networks. Social media used to coordinate attacks and spread disinformation about protesters.

headline: IFIT releases Kenya narrative assessment: Luo-Kikuyu polarization narratives intensifying ahead of 2027
summary: The Institute for Integrated Transitions published a major case study in August 2025 documenting dominant narratives shaping Luo-Kikuyu relations in Kenya, finding that even the Gen-Z protests had not escaped ethnic fault lines—with some participants accused of profiling Luo communities as politically unreliable. The study identified three dominant narrative trees: Kikuyu political dominance, Luo marginalization, and Kikuyu land acquisition as national control. Despite the 2024-2025 protests' initially tribeless character, ethnicity was increasingly being weaponized as 2027 approached.
actors: IFIT / Directorate of National Cohesion and Values
online_dynamics: Ethnic narratives amplified through social media influencers and political blogs. The Kikuyu-Luo rivalry framing was being deliberately reintroduced into Gen-Z protest discourse to delegitimize cross-ethnic solidarity.

headline: AUSSOM-Somali joint forces recapture Bariire, killing 100+ Al-Shabaab militants
summary: On August 1, AUSSOM troops (primarily Ugandan) in coordination with the Somali National Army launched an operation to recapture Bariire town in Lower Shabelle. Following a week-long ground offensive targeting militant positions, the forces regained control with more than 100 militants killed. This recapture represented the beginning of 'Operation Silent Storm' — a multi-phase campaign to retake key strategic towns. The success was heavily publicized by the government and AUSSOM as a counter-narrative to al-Shabaab's months of territorial gains.
online_dynamics: The Somali government and AUSSOM extensively publicized the Bariire recapture on social media as a major victory. NISA's coordinated communication with international media generated positive coverage. Al-Shabaab's networks downplayed the loss, claiming casualties were fabricated, in standard counter-narrative messaging.

sources.publisher: Security Council Report - Somalia October 2025 Monthly Forecast
AUSSOM-UPDF - Somali Forces Recapture Awdheegle Town

headline: Somali military court executes 6 Al-Shabaab members convicted of assassinations in August 2025
summary: Somalia's military court executed six Al-Shabaab members via firing squad in August 2025: three on August 8 (including a grenade thrower, surveillance agent, and assassin convicted of killings in Mogadishu and Lower Shabelle) and three more on August 14. Additionally, two Somali soldiers were executed for conspiring with al-Shabaab to assassinate a battalion commander. The government framed these executions as part of its judicial counter-terrorism strategy, signaling that even infiltrators within the security forces would face consequences. However, human rights observers noted concerns about fair trial standards.
online_dynamics: The executions were broadcast on state TV and publicized on Facebook and social media as government strength messaging. Al-Shabaab media networks challenged the legitimacy of the military courts, claiming the executions were religious persecution of Muslims. Human rights discourse on social media raised due process concerns.

sources.publisher: Garowe Online - Somalia Executes Three More al-Shabaab Members
TRT Afrika - Somalia executes soldiers for conspiring with Al Shabab
African Perceptions - Somali Military Court Executes Three Al-Shabaab Members

headline: Shahada News Agency reports AQAP Gaza aid in August 2025, weaving Somalia-Palestine jihadist narrative
summary: On August 19, 2025, Shahada News Agency — al-Shabaab's Arabic-language propaganda arm — published a report about AQAP sending humanitarian aid to Gaza, featuring a spokesperson for the obscure 'Ahrar Beit al-Maqdis' group. This content, as documented by FDD's Foundation for Defense of Democracies, sought to link al-Qaeda's East Africa branch (al-Shabaab) with the Palestinian cause, building recruitment appeal among Muslims globally grieved by the Gaza conflict. Shahada News Agency had been systematically using pro-Palestinian and Gaza content since October 2023 to expand al-Shabaab's appeal beyond Somalia to an international jihadist audience.
online_dynamics: Shahada News Agency's pro-Gaza propaganda generated engagement from non-Somali Arabic-speaking audiences, extending al-Shabaab's online reach beyond its traditional East African base. The FDD documented this strategy as a deliberate effort to broaden recruitment appeal. This content amplified the 'global Muslim oppression' narrative that al-Shabaab uses to justify its insurgency.

sources.publisher: FDD - Al Qaeda in the Arabian Peninsula donates aid to Gaza residents

headline: Al-Shabaab surrounds Harardhere coastal district capital in Mudug, threatening Houthi piracy link
summary: By late August 2025, al-Shabaab had dramatically expanded its presence around Harardhere in Mudug region — one of only two district capitals the government retained from its 2022 offensive. Militants surrounded the town from Galgadud, Hiraan, and Middle Shabelle, aiming to cut off the last SFG-controlled coastal hub with Indian Ocean access. Analysis showed Harardhere's capture would give al-Shabaab a port to re-engage Somali pirates and potentially receive Houthi weapons directly — creating a strategic arms smuggling node. Ma'awisley militia commanders threatened to withdraw due to unpaid salaries.
online_dynamics: Analytical outlets like Critical Threats and the Africa Center published detailed assessments that were amplified on social media, warning of the strategic implications. Al-Shabaab propaganda framed its Harardhere positioning as a natural progression of its 'liberation' campaign. Ma'awisley militia complaints about unpaid salaries circulated on Somali social media, highlighting government dysfunction.

sources.publisher: Critical Threats - JNIM Economic Warfare: Africa File, September 11, 2025
Security Council Report - Somalia October 2025 Monthly Forecast

headline: Information Minister warns of rise in hate speech, misinformation, disinformation on digital platforms
summary: Information Minister Ateny Wek Ateny publicly stated that South Sudan had seen a significant rise in hate speech, misinformation, and disinformation on digital platforms. The minister's statements came ahead of the September charges against Machar and marked escalating government concern about online ethnic mobilization. Civil society organizations noted the dual risk: genuine harmful speech combined with government conflation of political criticism with 'hate speech.'
online_dynamics: The minister's warnings were shared on social media but met with skepticism from digital rights advocates who noted the government itself was a major producer of inflammatory content. The statements served as a prelude to the November 2025 cybercrime bill passage.

headline: KDF kills five Al-Shabaab fighters in intelligence-led operation at Boni Forest camp, seizes weapons cache
summary: A Kenya Defence Forces Special Forces team killed five al-Shabaab fighters and injured others in an intelligence-led operation at the Lacta Mangai region of Boni Forest, Lamu County. The KDF seized AK-47 rifles, RPG launchers with warheads, ammunition, solar panels, tents, and IED-making materials from a makeshift camp. Separately, on August 27, KDF also thwarted an al-Shabaab attack on a security installation in Lamu County. These operations were part of the ongoing multi-agency Operation Amani Boni.
online_dynamics: KDF released official press statements. Al-Shabaab's media wing monitored but did not confirm operation details. Counter-terrorism success message amplified by Kenyan media to boost public confidence.

headline: EUAA 2025 Country Focus: Al-Shabaab recruitment through coercion, Koranic schools, and clan mediation throughout 2025
summary: The European Union Agency for Asylum's 2025 Country Focus on Somalia documented that al-Shabaab continued multidimensional youth recruitment through 2025: using Koranic schools for indoctrination (particularly in Mogadishu outer districts with high IDP populations), clan mediation as leverage (demanding recruits as payment for conflict resolution services), economic coercion, and ideological appeal. About 500 children were estimated recruited in 2023 alone (UNICEF); the practice continued through 2025. Al-Shabaab also exploited Somali anger at the Ethiopia-Somaliland MOU from 2024 for new recruitment. Puntland government complained that the FGS was 'covertly recruiting Puntland youth' for frontline deployment, creating inter-state recruitment tensions.
online_dynamics: Al-Shabaab used social media and Telegram to disseminate recruitment messaging to diaspora youth and Somalis in neighboring countries. The Ethiopia-Somaliland MOU anger was exploited in digital messaging to frame joining al-Shabaab as defending Somali sovereignty. NISA maintained counter-recruitment hotlines (199, 0770 747474, 0620 545454) publicized via social media.

sources.publisher: EUAA - Recruitment of men, women, and children by Al-Shabaab
CTC West Point - Global State of al-Qa'ida 24 Years After 9/11

headline: NISA kills al-Shabaab mastermind of March 18 presidential assassination attempt
summary: Somalia's NISA announced it killed Mohamed Abdi Dhiblawe Afrax (aliases: Khalid Dheere, Khalid Dacas, Macallin Siciid), identified as the mastermind behind the March 18 assassination attempt on President Hassan Sheikh Mohamud, in a targeted operation in Ugunji, Lower Shabelle. The elimination of the planner behind the highest-profile 2025 attack was widely publicized by NISA and the government as a major counter-terrorism success. The operation was conducted with international partners, signaling continued intelligence cooperation.
online_dynamics: NISA announced the killing with supporting details of Afrax's multiple aliases and his role in the March attack. The announcement received extensive social media engagement in Somalia. Al-Shabaab networks did not confirm the killing, maintaining information ambiguity. The government used the announcement to signal ongoing intelligence effectiveness.

sources.publisher: Hiiraan Online - Somali Intelligence killed Al-Shabaab commander behind assassination attempt
TRT Afrika - Somali forces kill mastermind of failed assassination attempt

headline: Machar charged with treason, murder, crimes against humanity; Kiir suspends him from VP role
