summary: Justice Minister Joseph Geng Akech announced on 11 September that Machar and seven co-defendants (all Nuer) faced charges of treason, murder, crimes against humanity, conspiracy, terrorism, and destruction of property related to the March 2025 Nasir attack. On the same day, President Kiir issued a decree suspending Machar as First Vice President. Machar's spokesperson called the charges a 'political witch-hunt.' The announcement confirmed international fears that Kiir was using the judiciary as a tool to eliminate political opposition.
online_dynamics: The charges were announced at a media briefing broadcast on SSBC and immediately went viral globally. SPLM-IO's response was distributed via Facebook, WhatsApp groups, and to international media. The ethnic composition of the accused (all Nuer) generated extensive online commentary about ethnic targeting. CNN and BBC coverage was shared thousands of times in diaspora communities.

headline: Machar trial begins on SSBC; Special Court rejects jurisdictional challenges; independent journalists blocked
summary: On 22 September 2025, Machar and co-defendants (all Nuer) appeared before a Special Court for National Crimes in nationally televised proceedings broadcast exclusively on SSBC. The trial began on 25 September after the court on 29 September rejected all jurisdictional challenges. The government gave SSBC unrestricted access while independent journalists and civil society were blocked from attending. The African Arguments journal published an analysis asking whether the trial was 'tribal or political.'
online_dynamics: SSBC broadcast generated extensive real-time commentary on Facebook, with government supporters celebrating legal accountability and opposition/diaspora accounts decrying a show trial. The ethnic composition of the accused was continuously highlighted online. Defyhatenow and civil society monitors documented increased hate speech around the trial broadcasts. Independent commentary from blocked journalists was shared via WhatsApp.

headline: Al-Shabaab frees detainees in Warta Nabadda jailbreak as NISA kills 22+ militants in concurrent raids
summary: On September 25, heavily armed assailants stormed Mogadishu's Warta Nabadda District Police Station with combat vehicles, freeing security detainees and inflicting significant casualties. The same day, NISA conducted concurrent counter-terror raids: killing senior commander Abdi Hiiray in Hiiraan, neutralizing 11 fighters in Galgaduud, and killing 10+ in Lower Shabelle. This juxtaposition illustrated Somalia's 'dual-front security challenge': simultaneous vulnerability in Mogadishu and growing precision strike capacity in rural areas. The jailbreak followed just days before the Godka Jilaow prison attack.
online_dynamics: The jailbreak generated alarm among Mogadishu residents on social media, with viral commentary questioning the government's ability to protect even police stations. NISA publicized the concurrent rural operations as a counter-message, but the urban security failure dominated online discourse. Al-Shabaab channels amplified the jailbreak as evidence of government vulnerability.

sources.publisher: African Security Analysis - Mogadishu Jailbreak Exposes Security Gaps

headline: UNMISS Q2 2025 report: 334 violent incidents, 1,518 victims; CRSV up 85%; conventional forces up 127%
summary: UNMISS published its quarterly brief on violence affecting civilians for April-June 2025, documenting 334 incidents affecting 1,518 civilians (635 killed, 676 injured, 133 abducted, 74 CRSV victims). Compared to the previous quarter, violence from conventional parties (SSPDF and armed groups) increased 127%. Compared to Q2 2024, total victims increased 43%. The report highlighted a significant increase in violence attributable to the conflict parties—not just community militias—signaling a qualitative shift in the nature of the violence.
online_dynamics: The UNMISS report was distributed via UN platforms and picked up by human rights organizations for social media campaigns. Numbers were shared widely in diaspora WhatsApp groups as evidence of systematic targeting. Government accounts disputed the figures or emphasized community militia attribution.

headline: South Sudan demands UNMISS reduce forces 70%, close bases; issues note verbale on Wau and Bentiu closure
summary: In October 2025, South Sudan's Ministry of Foreign Affairs formally requested UNMISS reduce its international military contingents by 70%, ground all intelligence-gathering aircraft, and close several military bases including civilian protection sites. In November, Juba issued a note verbale specifically calling for the closure of bases in Wau and Bentiu. Combined with concurrent budget-driven reductions, these demands significantly constrained UNMISS's ability to protect civilians amid escalating conflict.
actors: South Sudan Ministry of Foreign Affairs
online_dynamics: The UNMISS base closure demands were publicized by Radio Tamazuj and shared widely online. Human rights advocates condemned the demands as deliberate obstruction of civilian protection. Government accounts argued the demands were matters of sovereignty. The dispute dominated diplomatic discourse and was referenced in international advocacy campaigns.

headline: Al-Shabaab storms Godka Jilaow NISA prison with SVBIED, claims to free 'Muslim prisoners'
summary: On October 4, seven al-Shabaab attackers disguised in military uniforms stormed the Godka Jilaow underground NISA prison — located near the Villa Somalia presidential compound — using a suicide vehicle bomb followed by ground assault. Al-Shabaab claimed in a Telegram statement it launched 'Operation: Support for the Oppressed' to 'free all the Muslim prisoners the apostates had been holding and torturing.' While all 7 attackers were killed, dozens of prisoners escaped (some later recaptured). The attack — the 22nd suicide bombing in Somalia in 2025 — exposed a critical security failure near the presidential palace and hours after the government had reopened capital roads for the first time in 10 years.
online_dynamics: Al-Shabaab's Telegram claim framed the operation in explicitly religious terms (Muslim prisoners vs. 'apostate' state), which was amplified on diaspora social media. The attack came hours after government reopening of Mogadishu roads — maximizing psychological impact and political embarrassment. The claim of 'freed Muslim prisoners' had significant resonance in religious communities online.

sources.publisher: Reuters - Somali forces fighting al Shabaab attack on high-security prison
BBC - Al-Shabab militants dress as soldiers to storm Somali jail
Al Jazeera - Al-Shabab fighters killed in Mogadishu prison assault
FDD - Shabaab claims prison assault in Mogadishu

headline: Operation Silent Storm Phase 3: Somali-AUSSOM forces recapture strategic Awdheegle from Al-Shabaab
summary: On October 4-5, Somali National Army and Ugandan AUSSOM forces recaptured Awdheegle district in Lower Shabelle — a strategic junction connecting major routes to Mogadishu — in Phase 3 of Operation Silent Storm. The operation killed significant numbers of militants and recovered weapons including PKMs, RPGs, sniper rifles, and ammunition. The recapture came on the same day as the Godka Jilaow prison attack in Mogadishu, creating a contradictory narrative: government success in the field but vulnerability in the capital. AUSSOM framed the systematic recapture of Bariire (August 8), Sabiid/Anole (July 21), and now Awdheegle as a deliberate campaign.
online_dynamics: The simultaneous recapture of Awdheegle and the prison attack in Mogadishu dominated Somali social media. Government supporters amplified the military victory; opposition and al-Shabaab networks highlighted the prison attack. The split narrative exposed the limits of the government's ability to project a unified security message.

sources.publisher: Hiiraan Online - Somali forces and AU troops capture strategic Awdheegle district
UPDF - AUSSOM-UPDF And Somali Forces Recapture Awdheegle Town

headline: Ruto signs expanded Cybercrimes Amendment Act granting sweeping website-blocking powers without court oversight
summary: Parliament passed the Computer Misuse and Cybercrimes (Amendment) Act on October 8, 2025, with President Ruto signing it into law on October 15. The law granted the National Computer and Cybercrimes Coordination Committee (NC4)—composed largely of security officials—power to order ISPs to block websites or apps promoting unlawful activities, including terrorism, without requiring a court order. Rights groups including Article 19, Human Rights Watch, and BAKE condemned the law as unconstitutional, noting it could be used to suppress protest-related speech following the 2024-2025 Gen-Z protests.
online_dynamics: The law was seen as a direct response to TikTok and social media's role in organizing protests. Digital rights activists launched online campaigns opposing the law. High Court temporarily suspended key provisions on October 22, 2025.

headline: UN Commission: 300,000 people fled South Sudan in 2025; political transition 'falling apart'
summary: The UN Commission on Human Rights in South Sudan published a report confirming approximately 300,000 people had fled South Sudan in 2025 due to escalating violence. Commissioner Barney Afako stated 'the political transition in South Sudan is falling apart.' The Commission documented systematic corruption, leadership failure, aerial bombardments in civilian areas, and warned of a 'renewed slide into full-scale conflict.' Kiir suspended Machar from his role in early October.
actors: UN Commission on Human Rights in South Sudan (CHRSS)
online_dynamics: The UN report and Kiir's suspension decree were major online events. Al Jazeera's coverage was widely shared. Diaspora communities used the 300,000 displacement figure in fundraising and advocacy campaigns. Misinformation about the Commission's mandate and findings was actively promoted by government-aligned accounts.

headline: UN Panel of Experts confirms intensifying Houthi-al-Shabaab cooperation: weapons, drones, training
summary: A UN Security Council Panel of Experts report on Yemen (October 17, 2025) confirmed that cooperation between Houthi rebels and al-Shabaab 'involves weapons smuggling, technical training including drone technology, and exchange of logistical support.' The report stated Houthi-AS cooperation 'is not simply transactional but part of a Houthi strategy to wield increasing influence in the region.' Somali security services had intercepted multiple arms shipments including explosives and drones from Yemen. ISS-Somalia was also found to be receiving drone technology, and Houthis were training al-Shabaab in manufacture of sophisticated IEDs.
online_dynamics: Al-Shabaab's Shahada News Agency continued amplifying pro-Houthi narratives framing the Red Sea as a unified anti-Western resistance front. UN findings generated international media coverage of the Iran-Houthi-AS axis. Somali government used UN report to argue for continued international support against al-Shabaab.

sources.publisher: Africa Defense Forum - U.N. Report Shows Increasing Collaboration of Houthis, al-Shabaab
Stratfor - The Outlook for Cooperation Between Yemen's Houthis and Somalia's al Shabaab

headline: High Court suspends controversial cybercrime law provisions amid digital rights uproar
summary: Kenya's High Court issued conservatory orders on October 22, 2025, temporarily suspending Section 27 of the Computer Misuse and Cybercrimes (Amendment) Act—which broadened cyber harassment definitions and introduced up to KSh 20 million fines and 10 years imprisonment. The petition was filed by musician-activist Reuben Kigame and the Kenya Human Rights Commission, arguing the amendments were unconstitutional. The ruling provided a temporary reprieve for digital rights advocates and signaled judicial pushback against the post-protest digital crackdown.
online_dynamics: Court ruling celebrated by digital rights community on social media. Civil society used the ruling to push back against surveillance narrative. Government spokesperson framed it as routine judicial process.

headline: JHR launches counter-disinformation campaign; trains 1,000 community members on media literacy
summary: Journalists for Human Rights (JHR) launched a campaign targeting misinformation, disinformation, and hate speech in South Sudan, amid the ongoing political crisis around Machar's trial. The initiative trained approximately 1,000 community members and civil society actors on media literacy and engaged 700 government and security officials on coordinated action. Campaign videos highlighted the dangers of disinformation while promoting peace, accountability, and human rights.
online_dynamics: Campaign materials were distributed across Facebook and WhatsApp. The initiative operated against a backdrop of intense online hate speech around the Machar trial. JHR noted the challenge of counter-messaging in a high-tension political environment where audiences were highly polarized.

headline: Kenya hate speech rate at 43% of analyzed content — Just Security report reveals platform moderation retreat endangering speech environment
summary: A Just Security analysis published in late October/November 2025 documented that 43 percent of analyzed Kenyan content showed strong indicators of hate speech, particularly along ethnic and religious lines. The report noted that 26 percent of content reflected online violence trends. This coincided with Meta's January 2025 decision to roll back content moderation policies, which civil society groups argued was devastating for high-risk countries like Kenya where platform moderation prevented escalation of ethnic incitement into violence.
online_dynamics: Platform moderation retreat by Meta (announced January 2025) coincided with measured increase in ethnic incitement content. Local fact-checkers and civil society unable to fill the gap.

headline: South West State government warns against diaspora-driven clan hate speech and incitement online
summary: South West State's administration issued a formal warning against individuals — particularly outside conflict zones — spreading clan-based hate speech and inflammatory messaging that was fueling inter-clan violence in the region. The Ministry of Interior stated that some people living outside conflict areas were spreading inflammatory messages deepening clan divisions, and warned of legal action. The statement came amid a documented rise in inter-clan violence in 2024 (168 incidents vs 90 in 2023), particularly in Gedo, Lower Juba, and Shabelle regions. Diaspora amplification was specifically identified as an accelerant.
online_dynamics: Authorities highlighted that diaspora individuals physically removed from conflict were using social media to amplify and instigate clan confrontations. The 'Abgaalistan' rhetoric — calling for Abgal clan dominance in Mogadishu and its vicinity — was circulating on Somali social media during this period, reflecting broader clan-based political tensions. Officials appealed to media and social media users to act responsibly.

sources.publisher: Somali Magazine - Somalia's South West State Cracks Down on Hate Speech and Clan Incitement
ReliefWeb/ACAPS - Impact of clan conflicts in Somalia (March 2025)

headline: MPs warn of 2007-level ethnic violence risk as Nyeri Governor celebrates Odinga death online; NCIC accused of inaction
summary: Members of Parliament appearing before the Senate National Cohesion Committee warned that Kenya risked sliding back to 2007-style post-election violence due to unchecked ethnic incitement. The warning followed controversial remarks by Nyeri Governor Mutahi Kahiga, who appeared to celebrate a false report about the death of former PM Raila Odinga, claiming it was 'God's way' of stopping resources from going to Nyanza. Though the governor later apologized and resigned from the Council of Governors vice chairmanship, the remarks triggered a wave of ethnic attacks online. Senators accused NCIC of failing to take decisive action.
online_dynamics: Governor's remarks amplified by tribal political accounts. Online attacks targeting Luo community followed the false celebration. Tribal slurs circulated across X and TikTok. NCIC's response characterized as inadequate by senators.

headline: 'No longer tribeless': Nation Africa documents toxic ethnicity resurgence as 2027 election mobilization intensifies
summary: Nation Africa published a major analysis documenting how tribal rhetoric had re-emerged after a relatively tribeless Gen-Z protest movement in 2024. The report documented how 2025 saw political figures reintroducing community-based mobilization, with ethnic entrepreneurs exploiting the post-protest vacuum to rebuild tribal coalitions ahead of 2027. NCIC Commissioner Danvas Makori noted that 'Kenyans, including Gen-Zs, are intrinsically tribal and ethnic.'
online_dynamics: Facebook post by NCIC Commissioner Makori sparked debate. Nation Africa analysis widely shared, generating significant discussion about the failure of cross-ethnic solidarity.

headline: Senate orders NCIC to fire 22 irregularly hired staff; nepotism scandal undermines commission credibility
summary: The Senate Standing Committee on National Cohesion ordered NCIC to immediately terminate 22 staff hired irregularly by outgoing commissioners just before their October 2025 term ended, in positions that were never publicly advertised. NCIC CEO Dr. Daniel Giti testified he was pressured to comply with the illegal hiring. The scandal severely damaged the NCIC's credibility precisely as hate speech was rising ahead of the 2027 elections—undermining the commission's standing as an impartial arbiter of national cohesion.
online_dynamics: Scandal widely covered on Kenyan television and YouTube. Social media commentary framed the NCIC's hiring as proof of the 'captured institution' narrative, reducing public trust in hate speech enforcement.

headline: Amnesty International: Kenya authorities paid state trolls to suppress Gen-Z protests; deployed AI-generated disinformation and surveillance
summary: Amnesty International released its landmark report documenting systematic state-sponsored tech-facilitated violence against Gen-Z protesters between June 2024 and July 2025. The report documented paid troll networks (earning KES 25,000–50,000/day) countering protest hashtags in real time, AI-generated pornographic images targeting women activists, Islamophobic imagery targeting Somali-Kenyan journalist Hanifa Adan, and evidence of Safaricom-enabled surveillance of protesters. Secretary General Agnès Callamard confirmed this was state-driven, with over 128 deaths, 3,000 arrests, and 83 enforced disappearances across the protest period.
online_dynamics: Network of 20-person troll teams mass-posted identical messages to hijack X trending algorithm. Counter-hashtags (#RutoMustGoOn) deployed against protest hashtags (#RutoMustGo). AI-generated pornographic images sent to women activists to silence them. Doxxing of activists with children's school details used to threaten compliance.

headline: Al-Shabaab IED kills two Border Patrol Unit officers on Liboi-Kulan road, Garissa County
summary: Two Kenya Border Patrol Unit officers were killed when their vehicle struck an IED planted by al-Shabaab along the Liboi-Kulan road in Garissa County. The improvised explosive device destroyed their vehicle and killed both officers on the spot. This attack was part of a documented pattern of IED-based attacks in the Liboi-Kulan-Amuma-Hulugho corridor identified in Kenya's 2025 National Security report as one of the highest-risk terrorism corridors.
online_dynamics: Anadolu Agency reported the attack. Al-Shabaab claimed credit via Telegram. Local community WhatsApp groups spread fear-based messages about border insecurity.

headline: Parliament passes Cybercrime and Computer Misuse Bill amid rights concerns
summary: The Transitional National Legislative Assembly passed the Cybercrime and Computer Misuse Bill unanimously on 25 November 2025 following an extraordinary session. The bill criminalizes unauthorized data transmission, hacking, fake websites, false information publication, online impersonation, and gender-based harassment. Civil society and digital rights advocates warned the vague definitions of 'perilous acts' and broad surveillance powers could be used to suppress journalism, political opposition, and human rights defenders. Penalties include up to two years in prison or fines of 1 million SSP.
online_dynamics: The bill's passage was a major online news event. Rights advocates circulated critiques on social media, highlighting specific provisions enabling surveillance without court warrants (Section 6). Government supporters promoted the bill as necessary to combat hate speech. The bill was seen as a chilling effect instrument ahead of the 2026 elections—a concern widely aired on Facebook and in WhatsApp discussion groups.

headline: AFRICOM strikes ISIS-Somalia in Golis Mountains Nov 26-28 as 2025 total exceeds 109 strikes
summary: AFRICOM conducted multiple airstrikes targeting ISIS-Somalia on November 26-28 in the Golis Mountains, approximately 60km southeast of Bossaso. By this point in 2025, US airstrikes in Somalia exceeded 109 for the year — a dramatic escalation from the 2024 pace. The November 27 operation involved US Special Forces and Puntland Counter-Terrorism Forces in a joint pre-dawn raid on ISIS strongholds in Baalade Valley, killing a senior ISIS leader and 15 other militants. By November 2025, Puntland forces had besieged the last remaining ISIS stronghold in the Baalade valley.
online_dynamics: Each AFRICOM strike generated official press releases that were circulated on social media. ISS propaganda networks on Telegram continued to reframe each strike as evidence of US aggression and used them in fundraising appeals directed at diaspora sympathizers.

sources.publisher: AFRICOM - US Forces Conduct Strikes Targeting ISIS-Somalia
Ujasusi Blog - Tanzanian ISIS Fighters analysis

headline: NSS detains Al-Watan newspaper editor Michael Christopher; civil society raises alarm
summary: National Security Service agents detained Michael Christopher, editor-in-chief of the Arabic-language daily Al-Watan, at his Juba home on 27 November 2025. No official reasons were given. Civil society groups noted that Christopher had used Facebook to broadcast live political commentary, call for removal of senior officials, and express support for Sudan's RSF—blurring the line between journalism and political activism. The arrest raised acute concerns about NSS use of social media activity as grounds for detention under existing national security laws.
online_dynamics: Christopher's arrest was immediately publicized on social media by colleagues and civil society organizations. It was widely interpreted as a signal that Facebook live-streaming of political commentary could result in detention. The timing—immediately after the Cybercrime Bill passage—reinforced fears of escalating press freedom violations.

region: Kasipul, Malava, Mbeere North, Magarini, Ugunja, Baringo, Banissa
headline: IEBC condemns violence, intimidation and election disruption during November 27 by-elections across seven constituencies
summary: The Independent Electoral and Boundaries Commission (IEBC) condemned 'grave' incidents of violence, intimidation, and disruption reported across multiple by-election areas on November 27, 2025, including in Kasipul, Malava, and Mbeere North. KNCHR issued a human rights call urging adherence to constitutional principles. The elections coincided with rising 2027 ethnic mobilization narratives. IEBC rejected claims of irregularities as 'false and unfounded,' while condemning acts of 'hooliganism and lawlessness.'
online_dynamics: Election disinformation spread through WhatsApp chains. IEBC claims of irregularities countered with false accusation of vote manipulation circulated on Facebook and X. By-election monitoring showed continued ethnic voting patterns reinforcing 2027 tribal mobilization.

headline: Gachagua delivers explosive 'Kikuyus must lead Nairobi' speech in Kikuyu language, fueling tribal incitement debate
summary: Former Deputy President Rigathi Gachagua delivered a highly controversial speech claiming the Kikuyu community should hold leadership in Nairobi based on their business dominance in the city. Delivered in Kikuyu language, the speech was widely characterized as tribal incitement by critics but defended as economic fact by supporters. The speech intensified his political trajectory as an ethnic entrepreneur mobilizing the Kikuyu community against Ruto's government ahead of 2027. NCIC had previously warned Gachagua multiple times against such rhetoric.
online_dynamics: Speech widely shared on YouTube, generating over viral commentary. Attempted translation by commentator Mwafreeka became itself controversial. Pro-Gachagua networks amplified as community validation; critics flagged as hate speech across X and Facebook.

headline: Journalist Santo Jal Dut arrested by Presidential Guards outside Machar trial courtroom
summary: Journalist Santo Jal Dut was arrested on 1 December 2025 by members of the Presidential Guards at approximately 8:20 a.m. outside the courtroom where Machar's trial was being conducted. The journalist had been denied entry into the courtroom, consistent with the government's policy of blocking independent media from the trial. The arrest came as civil society organizations were raising alarms about the pattern of restricting independent coverage of the political trial.
online_dynamics: Civil society and media organizations shared news of the arrest widely on Facebook. The timing—immediately outside the Machar trial building—reinforced narratives about deliberate media suppression. Diaspora communities used the incident as evidence of judicial and press-freedom violations.

headline: SSPDF helicopter gunship hits MSF health facility in Pieri; additional airstrikes in Lankien
summary: On 3 December 2025, an SSPDF helicopter gunship struck the MSF Primary Health Care Center in Pieri, Uror County, Jonglei State. Staff and patients were unharmed. Additional airstrikes were observed in Lankien, where MSF also operates facilities, without direct infrastructure damage. MSF stated it was the only healthcare provider in Pieri serving 250,000 people. This attack was part of a documented pattern—MSF had experienced eight targeted attacks in South Sudan during 2025, forcing closure of Old Fangak and Ulang hospitals.
online_dynamics: MSF's statement was widely shared on social media. The attack preceded the December offensive by just weeks and was framed by opposition accounts as deliberate pre-offensive destruction of healthcare infrastructure. Government spokespeople denied targeting.

headline: Leaked Turkish intelligence report warns of sophisticated disinformation campaigns targeting Somalia's foreign partnerships
summary: A December 2025 Nordic Monitor report on a leaked Turkish intelligence assessment warned that Somalia was 'entering its most fragile period in a decade' and highlighted 'increasingly sophisticated disinformation campaigns aimed at undermining Somalia's foreign partnerships, including with Turkey.' The report documented that disinformation actors were targeting the Turkey-Somalia defense and energy partnership — which included sweeping hydrocarbons agreements submitted to Turkish parliament in April 2025 — through fabricated claims and coordinated narratives. This was separate from al-Shabaab propaganda: the disinformation appeared linked to state or quasi-state actors seeking to undermine Ankara's growing Somalia footprint.
online_dynamics: The disinformation targeted both Somali domestic audiences (questioning Turkey's motives) and international audiences (undermining Turkish-Somali cooperation). Turkey's growing military and economic presence generated organic conspiracy narratives online, which coordinated actors sought to amplify. Somalia and Turkey would formalize a media partnership in February 2026 specifically to counter this.

sources.publisher: Nordic Monitor - Turkish intelligence report warns of Somalia's fragility
Somali Guardian - Leaked Turkish intelligence report

headline: Meta Oversight Board overturns removal of 'tugeges' comment on Kenyan politics, highlighting platform moderation failures in ethnic contexts
summary: The Facebook Oversight Board ruled that Meta's automatic removal of a comment including the term 'tugeges' (a contested Kikuyu political slur/criticism) was overbroad, finding the term did not inherently create discriminatory exclusion in the context used. Meta had designated the word as a slur in January 2024 during 2022 election aftermath but removed it from its slur list in July 2025. The case highlighted platform moderation failures in Kenya's complex ethnic political landscape and prompted Meta to enhance local stakeholder engagement for slur designations.
actors: Kenya's National Coalition on Freedom of Expression and Content Moderation
online_dynamics: Oversight Board ruling generated broad discussion on free speech versus ethnic hate speech line-drawing in Kenya. NCIC had previously warned against 'tugeges' but stopped short of classifying it as hate speech.

headline: UN Security Council unanimously extends Al-Shabaab sanctions regime through November 2026
summary: The UN Security Council unanimously adopted Resolution 2806 (December 12, 2025), extending sanctions on weapons entering Somalia and authorization for member states to intercept arms shipments through November 2026. The resolution also renewed the Panel of Experts' mandate through December 2026. Members 'stressed particular concern over recognized links between Al-Shabaab and the Houthi militia in Yemen.' The resolution acknowledged a technical assessment of the arms embargo (S/2025/673) and maintained pressure on al-Shabaab's weapons supply chains.
online_dynamics: The resolution generated mainstream media coverage but limited social media engagement. Al-Shabaab networks dismissed it as ineffective. The explicit mention of Houthi-AS links in a Security Council resolution represented a significant diplomatic milestone in the transnational framing of the al-Shabaab threat.

sources.publisher: UN Press - Security Council Extends Al-Shabaab Sanctions Regime

headline: Al-Kataib releases proof-of-life video of Ukrainian UN pilot held hostage since January 2024
summary: On December 13, al-Shabaab's Al-Kataib media wing released a proof-of-life video of Ukrainian pilot Oleksandr (Oleynik Aleksandr), who had been contracted by the UN and was taken hostage after making an emergency landing in al-Shabaab territory in January 2024. In the video, the pilot describes himself as a 'prisoner of war' and calls on Ukrainian President Zelensky to use diplomatic channels for his release. The video was a deliberate propaganda maneuver to gain international attention, create diplomatic pressure, and project al-Shabaab's capacity to hold foreign nationals indefinitely.
platforms: International media (Garowe Online, Hostage Aid Worldwide)
online_dynamics: The video was released via Al-Kataib's Telegram channels and immediately picked up by international news agencies. Al-Shabaab used the foreign hostage as a propaganda tool to demonstrate international reach and to generate diplomatic pressure. Ukrainian and UN response was minimal publicly, denying al-Shabaab the negotiation leverage it sought.

sources.publisher: Tracking Terrorism - Al-Shabaab Releases Proof of Life Video of Ukrainian U.N. Pilot
Hostage Aid Worldwide - Al-Shabaab Releases Video of Ukrainian U.N. Pilot

headline: National Conference on Combating Hate Speech and Strengthening Social Cohesion opens in Juba
summary: State Ministers of Information from all ten states and three administrative areas convened in Juba for a three-day National Conference on Combating Hate Speech and Strengthening Social Cohesion. The conference—organized by the South Sudan Media Authority in collaboration with Journalists for Human Rights—called on citizens to reject hate speech, misinformation, and divisive narratives ahead of the 2026 elections. Speakers noted that 'hate speech amounts to 37% of factors triggering social friction.' EU Delegation, civil society, and media managers participated.
actors: Ministry of ICT and Postal Services (Ateny Wek Ateny)
online_dynamics: Conference proceedings were broadcast on SSEM TV and shared on Facebook. The conference framing—using the government platform to call for responsible expression—was praised by some and criticized by others as hypocritical given simultaneous press freedom violations. The 37% hate speech statistic circulated widely online.

headline: President Ruto launches National Policy on Ethnic Minorities and Marginalized Communities 2025-2035
summary: President Ruto launched Kenya's National Policy on Ethnic Minorities and Marginalized Communities 2025-2035, framed as a counter-measure to rising ethnic polarization. The policy was announced at a moment of elevated ethnic tensions, partly triggered by post-Gachagua impeachment Kikuyu-Kalenjin fault lines and growing 2027 mobilization. Civil society organizations cautiously welcomed the policy while noting it needed strong implementation mechanisms.
online_dynamics: Launch broadcast nationally and live-streamed. Reception mixed on social media—government supporters praised it while opposition framed it as election PR.

headline: US sentences Kenyan al-Shabaab operative Cholo Abdi Abdullah to life in prison for 9/11-style aircraft hijack plot
summary: Cholo Abdi Abdullah, a 34-year-old Kenyan national and al-Shabaab operative, was sentenced to two consecutive life terms by US federal court for conspiring to hijack a commercial airliner and crash it into a building in the United States. Abdullah had trained as a commercial pilot in the Philippines using al-Shabaab funds. His case highlighted al-Shabaab's global reach, financing capacity, and long-term operational planning that extends far beyond the Horn of Africa into international terrorism.
online_dynamics: Sentencing covered by US and Kenyan media. Case reinforced al-Shabaab's existential threat narrative. Online discussion in Kenya linked the case to ongoing domestic security concerns.

headline: Israel becomes first country to recognize Somaliland as independent state; Somalia condemns as 'existential threat'
summary: On December 26, Israel became the world's first UN member state to formally recognize Somaliland's independence, triggering an immediate sovereignty crisis and disinformation surge. Somalia's President Hassan Sheikh Mohamud condemned the move as an 'illegal aggression' and 'existential threat,' alleging Israel was planning to use Somaliland for Palestinian displacement and Red Sea military positioning. Emergency UNSC meeting on December 30 saw most members condemning the move; the US declined to follow Israel. Turkey's Erdogan called it 'unacceptable.' Somali social media erupted with anti-Israel protests, nationalist rallies, and disinformation about Israeli plans for Somali territory.
online_dynamics: The recognition triggered massive online disinformation: widely circulated false claims that Israel had agreed to resettle Palestinians in Somalia, that Somaliland would become an Israeli military base targeting Muslim nations, and that the US was secretly behind the move. Somali diaspora organized online protest campaigns. Pro-Somali-unity accounts used the crisis to amplify 'Abgaalistan' and clan-based nationalism. Al-Shabaab's Shahada News Agency exploited the narrative to frame the FGS as unable to protect Somali territorial integrity.

sources.publisher: Al Jazeera - Israel's Somaliland move all about strategic objectives
BBC - Why Israel's recognition of Somaliland as an independent state is significant
Fair Planet - Mixed reaction over Israel's recognition of Somaliland

region: Northern Jonglei State (Uror, Nyirol, Duk, Ayod counties)
headline: SPLA-IO launches December offensive in Jonglei; captures Yuai on January 2; 100,000+ displaced in 12 days
summary: On 29 December 2025, the SPLA-IO launched a major offensive across northern Jonglei State, overrunning government positions. On 2 January 2026, SPLA-IO forces captured Yuai, the administrative center of Uror County—a significant strategic blow. The offensive was timed to coincide with the dry season, when improved road conditions facilitated military movement after months of flood-induced immobility. By 9 January, fighting (including airstrikes) had displaced over 100,000 people, primarily women, children, and elderly, in what UNMISS described as some of the fastest displacement ever recorded in the country.
online_dynamics: SPLA-IO military communiques were distributed via Facebook and WhatsApp networks. The offensive was celebrated on opposition pages and condemned on government-aligned accounts. Videos of displaced civilians spread on TikTok and Facebook, generating both humanitarian appeals and ethnic mobilization calls. Mobile networks were cut in conflict zones, limiting independent reporting.

headline: Kenya records 61 terror incidents in 2025: 15 active shooter attacks, 20 IEDs, 9 kidnappings across northeast counties
summary: Kenya's Annual Report on the State of National Security (covering September 2024-August 2025) documented 61 terror-related incidents including 15 active shooter attacks, 20 IED explosions, and 9 kidnappings. A total of 119 people were affected across the most impacted counties—Mandera, Wajir, Garissa, and Lamu—with 84 security officers and 35 civilians injured. Al-Shabaab's activities included probing attacks targeting security agencies, critical infrastructure, and installations, with the group exploiting security gaps and technology to evolve its tactics.
online_dynamics: Al-Shabaab's media network documented operations through Telegram and affiliated websites. Report noted group's increasing use of technology and propaganda to recruit and conduct operations.

headline: Nation Africa documents 2025 online disinformation ecosystem threatening Kenya's 2027 elections: deepfakes, coordinated networks, hashtag weaponization
summary: Nation Africa's year-end investigation revealed a sophisticated cross-platform disinformation ecosystem active throughout 2025 targeting Kenya's 2027 elections. The investigation identified a coordinated network that repurposed digital assets to attack different political targets across ethnic and regional lines. The #43against1 hashtag (January 2025), deepfake videos, and AI-generated ethnic content were identified as central tools in the pre-election information warfare environment.
online_dynamics: Cross-platform coordinated activity documented. Networks repurposed assets for different political targets. Deepfakes and AI-generated content used to discredit politicians across ethnic lines. Early 2027 election positioning already shaping information space.

headline: AFRICOM strikes Al-Shabaab near Shabeellaha Dexe, ~64km northeast of Mogadishu
summary: AFRICOM conducted airstrikes targeting al-Shabaab on December 29, 2025, in the vicinity of Shabeellaha Dexe, approximately 64km northeast of Mogadishu. The strike was part of an escalating pace of operations as 2025 ended with US airstrikes dramatically higher than 2024 levels. This positioned the US for the January 2026 surge that would see 26 strikes in a single month.
online_dynamics: Routine strike announcements continued to generate Somali social media commentary about US military presence. Al-Shabaab networks attempted to portray each strike as foreign aggression on Somali soil. Government messaging emphasized partnership and coordination.

sources.publisher: AFRICOM - US Forces Conduct Strikes Targeting al-Shabaab (Dec 29, 2025)

headline: AFRICOM launches 26 strikes in January 2026 alone — 12x increase vs 2024 — targeting both Al-Shabaab and ISIS-Somalia
summary: In January 2026, AFRICOM conducted 26 airstrikes in Somalia, a twelvefold increase compared to 2024 figures, targeting both al-Shabaab and ISIS-Somalia across multiple regions. Confirmed targets included ISIS-Somalia in the Golis Mountains (Jan 1-3, Jan 8-9, Jan 12, Jan 23-24), al-Shabaab in Godane (Jan 3, 60km northeast of Mogadishu), and al-Shabaab near Kismaayo (Jan 18). On January 15, Somali/AUSSOM forces killed 30+ al-Shabaab in a joint operation near Bulunagaad base in Lower Shabelle. On January 18, Puntland forces publicly displayed captured Tanzanian, Ethiopian, Yemeni, Moroccan, Turkish, Saudi, and Syrian foreign ISIS fighters in Bosaso, confirming that 60-70% of ISS operational strength comprised foreign fighters.
online_dynamics: The 12x escalation was widely reported internationally and amplified on social media. FirstPost Africa and other outlets framed it as 'Trump bombs Somalia in self-defense.' Al-Shabaab and ISIS-Somalia networks used the surge in strikes for anti-US propaganda. The display of foreign fighters in Bosaso generated significant online coverage of ISS's transnational character. AFRICOM's public website listed all 2026 strikes by date.

sources.publisher: FirstPost Africa - Trump Escalates US Strikes on ISIS-Somalia, Al-Qaeda-Linked Al-Shabaab
AFRICOM - US Forces Conduct Strikes Targeting al-Shabaab (Jan 12)
AFRICOM - US Forces Conduct Strike Targeting ISIS-Somalia (Jan 23-24)
Ujasusi Blog - Tanzanian ISIS Fighters Captured
CFR - Conflict With Al-Shabaab in Somalia

headline: SPLA-IO captures Yuai (Uror County HQ), Pajut (Duk County), and Wau (Ayod County)
summary: SPLA-IO forces captured Yuai, the administrative center of Uror County, on 2 January 2026—the first major territorial gain of the new offensive. This was followed by the capture of Pajut in Duk County and Wau in Ayod County, consolidating opposition control across parts of northern Jonglei State. The territorial losses triggered a government response escalating into Operation Enduring Peace. Yuai's capture was described by SSPDF Chief of Defense Forces as the 'heart of the insurgency.'
online_dynamics: SPLA-IO publicized territorial gains on Facebook and through press releases distributed via WhatsApp. Government accounts initially downplayed the losses. The back-and-forth of territorial claims generated significant disinformation—with both sides publishing conflicting maps and casualty figures online.

headline: Gachagua links Somali community in Eastleigh to Minnesota fraud scandal at church; NCIC complaint filed
summary: Former Deputy President Rigathi Gachagua, speaking at AIPCA Kiratina Church in Kiambu on January 4, 2026, alleged that funds stolen in a Minnesota, USA fraud scandal were used to build a mall in Eastleigh, explicitly naming the Somali community as culpable. BBS Mall owners filed an NCIC complaint on January 6, with MMA Advocates arguing the remarks 'stigmatize law-abiding citizens, damage lawful businesses, and undermine national cohesion.' North Eastern MPs condemned the remarks as 'dehumanization.' The NCIC later forwarded an investigation file to the ODPP.
online_dynamics: Church speech recorded and widely shared on YouTube and Facebook. North Eastern MPs condemned the remarks in a widely-viewed YouTube press conference. Social media debate polarized along ethnic and religious lines.

headline: Atheists in Kenya leader Harrison Mumia charged under Cybercrimes Act for AI-generated images depicting Ruto as dead
summary: Harrison Mumia, president of Atheists in Kenya, appeared in court on January 5, 2026 facing charges under the Computer Misuse and Cybercrimes Act for sharing AI-generated images portraying President Ruto as dead, posted via a fake Facebook account and Mumia's Instagram. He denied the charges. The judge imposed strict bail conditions including a KES 1 million bond, cash option of KES 500,000, passport surrender, and travel restrictions. The case followed a pattern of Cybercrimes Act prosecutions against critics sharing AI-generated political satire.
online_dynamics: AI-generated images shared on social media platforms. Prosecution used CMCA's false publication provisions. Digital rights community rallied support online for Mumia.

headline: AFRICOM strikes Al-Shabaab near Godane, ~60km northeast of Mogadishu (Jan 12, 2026)
summary: AFRICOM struck al-Shabaab positions near Godane, approximately 60km northeast of Mogadishu, on January 12, 2026 — part of the January 2026 surge. The proximity of the target to Mogadishu (within striking distance used by al-Shabaab) highlighted the ongoing threat to the capital. The strike was one of at least 10 publicly announced in January 2026 targeting both al-Shabaab and ISIS-Somalia.
online_dynamics: Strike announcement generated standard social media coverage. The pattern of strikes near Mogadishu was used in government counter-narratives to show international partners were actively degrading the threat to the capital.

sources.publisher: AFRICOM - US Forces Conduct Strikes Targeting al-Shabaab (Jan 12)

headline: SNA-AUSSOM joint operation kills 30+ al-Shabaab militants near Bulunagaad, NISA kills landmine planter near Afgooye
summary: On January 15, 2026, Somali National Army forces, AUSSOM troops (Ugandan forces), and international partners killed more than 30 al-Shabaab militants in a joint ground-and-air operation near Bulunagaad Base in Lower Shabelle. Separately, NISA carried out a targeted operation near Afgooye killing a senior al-Shabaab operative responsible for planting landmines. These dual operations reinforced the pattern of parallel conventional military operations and precision NISA strikes that characterized the counter-offensive.
online_dynamics: Operations were publicized on official channels and amplified on government social media. The Afgooye landmine planter killing addressed al-Shabaab's IED tactics targeting civilians and supply routes. Government supporters amplified the operations on social media to counter narratives of continued al-Shabaab momentum.

sources.publisher: CFR - Conflict With Al-Shabaab in Somalia (Jan 15, 2026 entry)

headline: AFRICOM strikes Al-Shabaab ~45km northeast of Kismaayo in Lower Juba Valley (Jan 18, 2026)
summary: AFRICOM conducted an airstrike in the Lower Juba River Valley on January 18, 2026, targeting al-Shabaab approximately 45km northeast of Kismaayo. This strike in southern Somalia demonstrated the geographic breadth of the January 2026 escalation, which hit al-Shabaab positions from northern Puntland (ISIS-Somalia) to Lower Juba (al-Shabaab) and Middle Shabelle simultaneously.
online_dynamics: Strike in southern Somalia added to the geographic breadth of January operations. Jubaland regional officials reinforced government messaging on social media.

sources.publisher: AFRICOM - US Forces Conduct Strike Targeting al-Shabaab (Jan 18)

headline: SPLA-IO calls for march on Juba; SSPDF commander issues 'crush the rebellion in 7 days' order
summary: On 19 January 2026, SPLA-IO Deputy Chief of Staff Wisley Welebe Samson instructed all SPLA-IO troops to 'advance to Juba from all regions of South Sudan and dismantle the anti-peace regime.' The same day, SSPDF Army Chief Paul Nang Majok gave soldiers seven days to 'crush the rebellion' in Jonglei. These escalatory statements were authenticated by UNMISS and the CHRSS, which expressed 'grave alarm' about inflammatory rhetoric from senior military figures and reports of forced mobilization. Both sets of rhetoric spread virally across social media.
online_dynamics: The competing commands—'advance on Juba' vs 'crush in 7 days'—generated a massive online reaction. Ethnic mobilization posts flooded Facebook within hours, with Dinka and Nuer community networks sharing content framing the conflict in existential terms. UNMISS and CHRSS condemnations were shared by international civil society. The BBC's 'army chief gives soldiers seven days' headline was among the most shared South Sudan stories of January 2026.

headline: Al-Shabaab's Mahad Karate resurfaces with October 2025 propaganda speech targeting Somalia and Kenya; Jamestown documents Kenya operational threat
summary: Jamestown Foundation analysis documented that al-Shabaab deputy emir Mahad Karate (Abdirahman Mohamed Warsame), with a $10 million US bounty, had resurfaced in October 2025 praising an attack on Somali intelligence and urging young Somalis to join al-Shabaab. The Jamestown analysis (published January 2026) specifically flagged Karate's central role in Kenya operations—including the 2015 Garissa University massacre—and noted that his intelligence expertise made him a key driver of complex operations in Kenya and high-impact bombing campaigns.
online_dynamics: Speech distributed via Telegram and al-Shabaab's media network. Propaganda explicitly targeted young Somalis and Kenyans for recruitment. Jamestown analysis amplified Karate's ongoing threat as high-value target for Kenya operations.

headline: General Johnson Olony issues 'spare no lives—not even a chicken' order; UNMISS authenticates audio; genocide accusations erupt online
summary: On approximately 24 January 2026, SSPDF Deputy Chief for Mobilization General Johnson Olony—former militia leader and Agwelek commander—issued an audio directive to forces in Jonglei urging them to 'spare no lives… not even the elderly… not even a chicken,' and to destroy civilian homes, livestock, and property. UNMISS authenticated the recording. A South Sudanese parliamentarian (Wany Bum Makhor of Jonglei) publicly called for Olony's removal, accusing him of inciting genocide. SPLM-IO described the statement as 'an early indicator of genocidal intent.'
online_dynamics: The authenticated audio went viral within hours—widely shared on Facebook and WhatsApp in both South Sudan and diaspora communities. On government-aligned pages, supporters minimized the statement as 'morale boosting' language. On Nuer community and opposition pages, it was framed as an explicit genocide order. The government tried to distance itself by calling the remarks 'inappropriate' and a 'slip of the tongue,' but did not take disciplinary action against Olony. International human rights organizations published alerts linking the statement to past atrocity patterns.

headline: SSPDF orders civilians, humanitarian workers, and UNMISS to evacuate three Jonglei counties in 48 hours
summary: On 25 January 2026, the SSPDF ordered all civilians in Nyirol, Uror, and Akobo counties in Jonglei State to immediately evacuate to 'government-controlled areas.' Humanitarian organizations were given 48 hours to leave. The order preceded the announced Operation Enduring Peace offensive. UNMISS stated its peacekeepers in Akobo would remain under their mandate to alleviate tensions. The combined displacement from 29 December to 19 January had already reached approximately 180,000 people.
online_dynamics: The evacuation order generated extensive international media coverage shared on social media. Humanitarian organizations posted urgent appeals on Facebook and Twitter. Civilian protests in Akobo against the order were filmed and shared on social media. The directive was framed by many online observers as preparation for mass civilian targeting.

headline: Government announces acquisition of 'device' to identify and track online hate speech perpetrators
summary: Information Minister Ateny Wek Ateny announced on 24 January 2026 at a Juba public gathering that the government had acquired 'a device capable of identifying individuals who use social media platforms to spread hate speech and incite violence.' He warned that even lending a phone to someone who uses it to spread hate speech would result in the phone owner being held accountable. Digital rights organization The ClarityDesk fact-checked and debunked the claim, noting no single device can track and attribute online speech, and that the announcement represented a deliberate chilling effect strategy ahead of elections.
online_dynamics: The announcement itself was reported by Eye Radio and spread rapidly on Facebook, generating significant fear among online users. Many South Sudanese reportedly began self-censoring political commentary. The ClarityDesk's debunking circulated in civil society and diaspora channels. The announcement was widely interpreted as a deliberate intimidation tactic—creating a chilling effect regardless of technical capability.

headline: HRW warns of atrocity risk in renewed civil war; 180,000 displaced; OHCHR documents Olony statement
summary: Human Rights Watch published a major alert warning that 'the risk of atrocities looms in South Sudan amidst renewed civil war,' citing key army officials ramping up 'incendiary rhetoric amid ethnic based mobilization,' aerial bombardments, and the documented Olony 'spare no lives' statement. By 27 January, 180,000 people had been displaced in Jonglei since the December offensive, rising to over 230,000 within days. OHCHR documented the Olony audio as part of a broader pattern of inflammatory rhetoric increasing atrocity risk.
online_dynamics: HRW's alert generated massive international media coverage shared extensively on social media. The combination of verified atrocity rhetoric (Olony audio) and displacement statistics made this one of the most-shared South Sudan stories globally. Government accounts dismissed HRW findings while opposition and diaspora pages used them to call for international intervention.

headline: SSPDF launches Operation Enduring Peace; announces offensive to retake northern Jonglei
summary: On 27-28 January 2026, the SSPDF formally launched 'Operation Enduring Peace'—a major counteroffensive to retake opposition-held territories in northern Jonglei State. The operation was announced publicly alongside the evacuation orders for three counties. The stated objective was to reclaim towns captured by SPLA-IO and 'reinstate law and order.' The operation involved ground forces, aerial bombardments, and deployed the Agwelek militia under General Olony. By February, the operation had displaced over 280,000 people in Jonglei.
online_dynamics: Government accounts framed Operation Enduring Peace as a legitimate anti-rebellion operation. Opposition and diaspora accounts framed it as a genocidal campaign against Nuer communities in northern Jonglei, pointing to the Olony statements and aerial bombardments. The operation name itself became a contested term online—peace vs. violence—generating disinformation about military objectives and civilian impact.

headline: SSPDF airstrike destroys MSF Lankien hospital; 26 MSF staff missing; Pieri facility looted
summary: In the early hours of 3 February 2026, an SSPDF airstrike struck the MSF hospital in Lankien, Nyirol County, destroying the main warehouse and all medical supplies. The same day, MSF's health facility in Pieri was looted by unidentified assailants. MSF staff evacuated the compound before the strike but 26 of 291 staff in Lankien and Pieri remained missing and uncontactable for weeks. The hospital had served an estimated 250,000 people. SSPDF simultaneously announced the 'defeat' of SPLA-IO in northern Jonglei.
online_dynamics: MSF's missing staff crisis generated global media attention and social media campaigns using the hashtag #ProtectHealthCare. The SSPDF simultaneously claiming 'victory' in northern Jonglei while MSF lost 26 staff created a stark disinformation context. Government denied deliberate targeting while MSF noted it had shared GPS coordinates with all parties.

headline: General Johnson Olony apologizes for 'spare no lives' remarks after public backlash
summary: On approximately 4-5 February 2026, General Johnson Olony formally apologized for his 'spare no lives' remarks, stating 'I regret those words.' Speaking after government forces had regained control of parts of Jonglei, Olony acknowledged that his earlier statement telling soldiers to 'spare no one, not even a chicken' was 'reckless and unnecessary.' He announced a disarmament campaign and called on SPLA-IO to 'seek power through elections.' The apology came after condemnation from parliamentarians, civil society, international human rights organizations, and the UN.
online_dynamics: The apology itself circulated widely but was received with deep skepticism by Nuer community and diaspora accounts on Facebook. Many online commentators noted the apology came after military operations had already occurred, without any accountability mechanism. Government-aligned pages highlighted the apology as evidence of self-correction; opposition pages argued the damage was done and operations had already resulted in civilian harm.

headline: Somali military court executes two al-Shabaab militants by firing squad in Mogadishu
summary: Farhan Mohamud Abdinur Aden and Abdi Abdulqadir Mohamed were executed by firing squad on February 10, 2026, following military court convictions for al-Shabaab activities. The executions were part of an ongoing judicial counter-terrorism strategy that saw multiple executions through 2025-2026. The government framed these as evidence of a functioning rule of law, while international human rights observers continued to raise concerns about the quality of fair trial guarantees in Somalia's military court system.
online_dynamics: Execution announcements were published on state media and circulated on Facebook. Al-Shabaab's online networks framed the executions as evidence of government persecution of Muslims, using them for religious grievance narratives targeting potential recruits.

sources.publisher: Eastleigh Voice - Somali military court executes two Al-Shabaab militants

headline: UN Security Council press statement expresses 'grave concern' at Jonglei escalation; warns of war crimes accountability
summary: The UN Security Council issued a press statement on 13 February 2026 expressing 'grave concern' at escalating violence in Jonglei and Eastern Equatoria. Council members explicitly recalled that 'individuals who order the commission of war crimes may be held accountable under international law,' a direct reference to Olony's statements and aerial bombardment patterns. The statement called on all parties to de-escalate, urged the South Sudanese government to cooperate with UNMISS, and raised concerns about UNMISS base closures disrupting the mission's protection mandate.
online_dynamics: The UNSC statement was shared extensively by civil society and diaspora accounts as evidence of growing international concern. Government accounts downplayed the war crimes warning. The explicit accountability language was highlighted by human rights organizations in social media campaigns.

headline: Vincent Ayomo (28) shot dead at Linda Mwananchi rally in Kitengela; police fire live ammunition at political gathering
summary: A 28-year-old man, Vincent Ayomo, was shot dead through the eye by a bullet while returning home from work when police fired on crowds at the Linda Mwananchi rally led by Nairobi Senator Edwin Sifuna in Kitengela, Kajiado County. IPOA launched an independent inquiry. The killing occurred when police deployed tear gas against thousands of supporters at what was billed as a peaceful opposition rally. Sifuna blamed the Ruto administration for the killing, calling it 'senseless.' Interior CS Murkomen later told Parliament that 579 people had been killed in mob justice since January 2025, partly due to politicians arming gangs.
online_dynamics: Killing documented on video and shared widely on social media. ODM and Linda Mwananchi networks amplified calls for justice. Government accused of using police to suppress political opposition.

headline: Al-Shabaab Ramadan mass-casualty plot foiled: 13 arrested in Kajiado with weapons cache; two Tanzanians among suspects
summary: Kenya's National Intelligence Service (NIS), in a multi-agency operation involving the Special Operations Group, arrested 13 suspects on the night of February 17, 2026 at a hideout in Kajiado County. The cell—10 Kenyans, 2 Tanzanians, 1 Ugandan—was rooted in the Dadaab refugee complex and was planning coordinated mass-casualty attacks on Nairobi during Ramadan. Seized weapons included five AK-47 rifles with 600 rounds of ammunition, pistols, grenades, explosives, and drugs. The Financial Reporting Centre simultaneously froze the suspects' accounts under terrorist financing provisions. The Counter Terrorism Policing Unit described 'months of surveillance' behind the operation.
online_dynamics: Plot finalized at physical hideout but cell reportedly used encrypted messaging. Al-Shabaab's Dadaab-based recruitment network implicated. Council on Foreign Relations and international media documented as 'Nairobi Intercepts Ramadan Plot' on February 20, 2026.

headline: AFRICOM strikes Al-Shabaab in Lower Shabelle as February 2026 campaign continues
summary: AFRICOM conducted an airstrike targeting al-Shabaab on February 17, 2026. The ongoing strike campaign in early 2026 continued the pace established in January, with multiple strikes across Middle Shabelle, Lower Shabelle, and Lower Juba, targeting al-Shabaab positions as the group continued its territorial pressure near Mogadishu.
online_dynamics: Each strike generated standard AFRICOM press release coverage. Al-Shabaab and sympathizer accounts continued to use strikes in anti-government and anti-US narratives.

sources.publisher: AllAfrica - AFRICOM conducts airstrike targeting al-Shabaab

headline: President Kiir signs Cyber Crime and Computer Misuse Act 2026 into law
summary: On 18 February 2026, President Salva Kiir signed the Cyber Crime and Computer Misuse Act 2026 into law, along with the Audit Chamber Amendment Act and the Wildlife Conservation Act. The law criminalizes unauthorized data transmission, hacking, fake websites, publication of false information, online impersonation, and gender-based harassment. Despite government framing as a tool against online hate speech and cybercrime, civil society warned the vague provisions enabling surveillance without court warrants (Section 6) could be weaponized against journalists, human rights defenders, and political opposition, particularly ahead of elections.
online_dynamics: The signing ceremony was broadcast live on state media and YouTube. Rights organizations and civil society groups posted warnings on Facebook and Twitter about the law's implications. The ClarityDesk and digital rights advocates circulated analyses of problematic provisions. The law was widely expected to be used as a tool for political prosecution given the simultaneous hate speech surveillance device announcement.

headline: National Assembly adopts motion for TikTok regulation ahead of 2027 elections, rejecting outright ban
summary: Kenya's National Assembly adopted a committee report calling for strict regulation of TikTok rather than an outright ban, following a 2023 petition citing child safety, data privacy, and content concerns. Critics and digital rights experts noted the timing—18 months before the 2027 elections—was suspicious, given TikTok's central role in organizing the 2024-2025 Gen-Z protests. Digital rights advocates warned the regulations could stifle free expression and be weaponized against government critics ahead of elections.
online_dynamics: TikTok debate itself heavily debated on TikTok and X. MPs cited 4.2 million new Kenyan TikTok users as justification. Critics framed regulation as 'narrative control' ahead of elections.

headline: Milimani Court acquits student David Mokaya of charges for AI-generated image of President Ruto's coffin
summary: 24-year-old student David Oaga Mokaya was acquitted by the Milimani Law Courts on February 19, 2026, with prosecutors failing to prove he was responsible for posting an AI-generated image of President Ruto's coffin in 2024. The ruling was part of a series of legal victories against the government's prosecution of political satire and AI-generated content, alongside the Rose Njeri charge dismissal in June 2025 and the broader CMCA constitutional challenge. The acquittal was seen as a signal that courts were pushing back against overbroad Cybercrimes Act prosecutions.
online_dynamics: Acquittal celebrated widely on digital rights social media accounts. Continued pattern of prosecutions under CMCA documented alongside series of acquittals.

region: Pankor village, Ayod County, Jonglei State
headline: Massacre of at least 16-21 civilians in Pankor village; Western nations express outrage; SSPDF admits involvement
summary: On 21 February 2026, at least 16-21 unarmed civilians including children were killed in Pankor village, Ayod County, Jonglei State. Witnesses described soldiers ordering civilians to assemble before opening fire. The government initially blamed 'unruly elements from the SSPDF soldiers' but denied deliberate targeting. In a joint statement on X, the US, EU, UK, and other Western nations expressed being 'appalled by credible reports of a deliberate massacre of civilians.' UNMISS and OHCHR head Volker Türk referenced the massacre in his Human Rights Council address, giving a higher count of 21 victims.
online_dynamics: The joint Western statement on X was amplified extensively by South Sudanese diaspora and human rights organizations. Government spokesperson Ateny argued casualties were crossfire. The SSPDF's subsequent admission that officers were involved (March 4) generated additional online outrage. The massacre reinforced the Olony 'spare no lives' narrative, with many online commentators arguing it was a direct consequence of the rhetoric.

headline: Violence erupts at Linda Mwananchi rallies in Siaya and Vihiga; second man George Olande killed by mob in Mbale
summary: Violence erupted at multiple Linda Mwananchi rallies on February 21, 2026. In Siaya, the 'Linda Ground' rally (counter-faction) faced clashes amid political rift within ODM. In Mbale, Vihiga County, George Olande was killed when a mob attacked him during a Linda Mwananchi meeting, with the post-mortem showing severe head injuries. Two families—Ayomo's from Kitengela and Olande's from Kisumu—simultaneously demanded justice for their relatives killed at political events within a week. Families also claimed security protocols were breached at Kisumu International Airport, with guns allegedly brought in for a political rally in Kakamega.
online_dynamics: Killings documented on video shared widely. ODM internal split amplified across political social media. Allegations of airport security breach and political gun-running generated significant social media outrage.

headline: AFRICOM conducts 6 days of consecutive ISIS-Somalia strikes in Golis Mountains (Feb 22-26, 28)
summary: AFRICOM conducted a concentrated multi-day airstrike campaign against ISIS-Somalia from February 22-26 and February 28, 2026, targeting militants approximately 70km southeast of Bossaso in the Golis Mountains. This represented the most sustained US air campaign against ISS in a single period and came alongside ongoing Puntland ground operations that had besieged the last ISS strongholds in the Baalade Valley.
online_dynamics: ISS propaganda channels responded to the multi-day strikes with intensified recruitment messaging targeting Ethiopian and Kenyan Muslim audiences, framing the campaign as US-backed 'crusader war on Muslims' in Puntland. The sustained nature of the campaign generated mainstream international media attention about ISS's resilience.

sources.publisher: AFRICOM - US Forces Conduct Airstrikes Targeting ISIS-Somalia (Feb 22-26, 28)

headline: NISA kills 23+ Al-Shabaab militants in four coordinated Hirshabelle operations, destroys Moqokori camp
summary: Somalia's NISA announced on February 24, 2026 that it conducted four planned security operations across Hiiraan and Middle Shabelle, killing more than 23 al-Shabaab terrorists who were preparing to launch attacks. Operations targeted fighters in Waab-Weyn, Goobo (near Mahaas), and Maxaa Saciid areas, and destroyed a militant camp in Moqokori where Hisbah forces were stationed. NISA stated the operations came after intelligence indicating al-Shabaab was mobilizing for Ramadan attacks — maintaining pressure on the group during a period historically associated with al-Shabaab offensive activity.
online_dynamics: NISA publicized the operations through official channels and state media. The mention of Hisbah units being targeted addressed al-Shabaab's shadow enforcement apparatus — generating social media discussion about AS's governance structures. Al-Shabaab networks disputed casualty figures.

sources.publisher: Somali National News Agency - NISA Kills Over 23 Al-Shabaab Militants
TRT World - Somalia's spy agency kills 23 Al Shabab members
Xinhua - Security forces kill 23 al-Shabab militants in central Somalia

headline: IG Kanja reports 12 joint NPS-NCIC hate speech investigations to Senate; warns of rising ethnic incitement ahead of 2027
summary: Inspector General Douglas Kanja appeared before the Senate Standing Committee on National Cohesion on February 25, 2026, reporting that the National Police Service and NCIC had investigated 12 cases of hate speech, incitement to violence, cyber harassment, and dissemination of inflammatory content over the past year. Most cases remained under investigation; a few had advanced through the legal process. Kanja outlined NPS's forensic lab capabilities for tracking inflammatory content and promised deepened collaboration between NPS, NCIC, and the ODPP ahead of the 2027 elections.
online_dynamics: 12 cases under investigation predominantly relate to social media content. NPS forensic lab tracking inflammatory statements and authenticating online content. Senate committee expressed concern that enforcement pace was insufficient.

headline: Interior CS Murkomen: 579 killed in mob justice since January 2025; politicians arming gangs for political violence
summary: Interior Cabinet Secretary Kipchumba Murkomen revealed to the Senate that 579 people had been killed in mob justice incidents since January 2025, with Kirinyaga County leading (58 deaths), followed by Nairobi (55), Kisumu (36), and Embu (33). Murkomen directly attributed the surge partly to politicians mobilizing violent gangs and arming youth for political events, including recent incidents in Kitengela, Kakamega, and Vihiga. He noted that police operations against these gangs were often complicated by interference from political leaders during rallies.
online_dynamics: Senate hearing widely covered on YouTube. Social media framed as evidence of political-gang nexus driving violence. Opposition parties used the data to attack the government's security record.

headline: Somalia and Turkey formalize structured media partnership to counter disinformation targeting bilateral cooperation
summary: Somalia and Turkey announced a formal anti-misinformation media partnership on February 25, 2026, directly responding to documented disinformation campaigns targeting Turkish-Somali cooperation. The agreement established structured media cooperation mechanisms to identify and counter false narratives about Turkish activities in Somalia — including about Turkey's military presence, hydrocarbons agreements, and security partnerships. The announcement came amid ongoing disinformation from both state and non-state actors, including al-Shabaab narratives about 'foreign occupation' and unidentified actors targeting Turkish-Somali energy deals.
online_dynamics: The partnership announcement was reported by pro-government media outlets. Critics and opposition figures on Somali social media questioned whether the partnership was more about controlling media narratives than genuine counter-disinformation work. The Turkey-Somalia energy agreement (hydrocarbons MoU from April 2025) remained a target of both domestic criticism and external disinformation.

sources.publisher: Goobjoog News - Somalia, Turkey Agree Structured Media Partnership to Counter Disinformation
Mareeg Media - Somalia, Turkey Reach Anti-Misinformation Deal

headline: Journalist Peter Mwita charged under Cybercrimes Act for WhatsApp message; Court of Appeal ruling on law's constitutionality imminent
summary: Journalist Peter Maseke Mwita was charged on February 25, 2026 after being detained for seven days (arrested December 31, 2025) for a WhatsApp message alleging links between a Mombasa politician and a criminal gang. CPJ documented Mwita as the latest in a series of bloggers, journalists, and activists prosecuted under the Cybercrimes Act. On February 27, Kenya's Court of Appeal was due to rule on the constitutionality of the CMCA following a 2018 petition by BAKE. Software developer Rose Njeri's May 2025 arrest and student David Mokaya's acquittal for posting an AI-generated Ruto image were also referenced as CMCA misuse cases.
online_dynamics: WhatsApp message about local politician became basis for prosecution. CPJ documented pattern of CMCA weaponization against critics. Court case generated major digital rights community attention on social media.

headline: OHCHR/Türk warns South Sudan at 'dangerous point'; 189 civilians killed in January alone—45% surge; CHRSS: 'leaders driving violence'
summary: UN Human Rights High Commissioner Volker Türk addressed the Human Rights Council on 27 February 2026, warning South Sudan was at 'a dangerous point' with rising hate speech and incitement targeting entire communities, an authenticated audio of a senior military official urging forces to 'spare no lives,' aerial bombardments in civilian areas, and a documented surge. OHCHR documented 189 civilians killed in January 2026—a 45% increase over December. Separately, the UN Commission on Human Rights in South Sudan (CHRSS) published a report finding 'political and military leaders are driving violence.' The conflict killed or injured 5,100+ in 2025, a 40% increase over 2024.
online_dynamics: Türk's speech and the CHRSS report were major international events generating extensive online coverage. The specific statistics (189 killed, 45% surge, 5,100 in 2025) were widely shared as evidence of systematic failure. Government-aligned accounts challenged the UN's impartiality. The CHRSS finding that leaders were 'driving violence' was used by opposition accounts to call for international sanctions.

headline: MPs accuse NCIC of being 'soft' on Gachagua hate speech case and political gang incitement; NCIC forwards Gachagua file to ODPP
summary: Members of the National Assembly Committee on Cohesion and Equal Opportunities accused NCIC of failing to decisively rein in politically sponsored gangs and Gachagua's unchecked hate speech. NCIC CEO Daniel Giti confirmed the commission had forwarded the Gachagua investigation file (relating to anti-Somali church remarks) to the Office of the Director of Public Prosecutions. Committee also raised concerns about county peace committees being 'breeding grounds for conflict.' Giti defended the commission's record while appealing to political leaders to act as 'ambassadors of peace.'
online_dynamics: Parliamentary hearing covered on Kenyan television. Social media debate about NCIC's effectiveness. Gachagua's anti-Somali remarks and failure to prosecute generated significant online outrage from Somali-Kenyan communities.

headline: Attack on Ruweng villages kills at least 169; 90 civilians among dead including women, children, elderly
summary: On Sunday 1 March 2026, unidentified armed men attacked multiple villages in Abiemnom County, Ruweng Administrative Area, killing at least 169 people including 90 civilians (women, children, and the elderly) and approximately 79 government soldiers. Local authorities reported victims were buried in mass graves. Initial reports cited by AFP suggested a Nuer group may have been retaliating for deaths of traders; the government denied this framing. The government declared security 'stabilized' while UNMISS temporarily sheltered approximately 1,000 civilians at its Ruweng base.
online_dynamics: The massacre generated significant online debate about ethnic attribution. Government accounts were accused of favoritism in how they condemned attacks (DW Africa noted 'the government is being accused of taking sides and showing favoritism to Dinka in the way it condemns attacks on civilians'). UNMISS's sheltering of displaced civilians was shared as a counter-narrative on social media. Mass grave imagery spread via WhatsApp.

headline: SSPDF issues evacuation order for Akobo town and surrounding areas; UNMISS ordered to shut down base
summary: On 6 March 2026, SSPDF issued an evacuation order for Akobo town and surrounding villages, ordering all civilians, NGOs, and UNMISS peacekeepers to leave within 72 hours ahead of a planned military offensive against the opposition stronghold. Akobo—the last major SPLM-IO-held town and under opposition control since 2013—was hosting over 82,000 displaced people and had been considered a relatively stable refuge. Mass civilian departures began immediately following the order, with thousands crossing into Ethiopia.
online_dynamics: The evacuation order was immediately covered globally and shared widely on social media. Civilian protests in Akobo against the order were documented and shared on Facebook and WhatsApp. The order was widely criticized online as forcing civilians to choose between flight (across a border) or remaining under bombardment. UNMISS's initial non-compliance became a major international story.

headline: Court of Appeal strikes down Cybercrimes Act false news provisions used against bloggers and activists — partial victory for free speech
summary: Kenya's Court of Appeal struck down Sections 22 and 23 of the Computer Misuse and Cybercrimes Act on grounds of legal uncertainty, declaring them unconstitutional for criminalizing false news in terms vague enough to encompass satire, opinions, and journalistic inaccuracies. The court upheld provisions on cyber harassment and surveillance. The ruling arose from BAKE's 2018 petition. While welcomed by rights groups including IPI and CPJ, they noted it was a partial victory—provisions on cyber harassment and surveillance remained, and authorities were urged to withdraw all pending cases against journalists.
online_dynamics: Ruling celebrated on social media as landmark win. Bloggers and HRDs who had faced prosecution under CMCA were among most vocal celebrants online. Government remained silent on pending cases.

headline: MSF forced to evacuate Akobo; Akobo hospital looted; pharmacy and medical supplies destroyed
summary: On 7 March 2026, the MSF team in Akobo was forced to evacuate following the SSPDF evacuation order, leaving hundreds of thousands of people without access to essential primary healthcare. The Akobo hospital (where MSF supported the pediatric ward) was subsequently looted, along with MSF's pharmacy, resulting in the loss of all medicines and medical supplies. MSF's office was also vandalized by unidentified attackers. MSF had been the primary provider for approximately 112,000 people and had recently resumed operations after government-imposed flight restrictions.
online_dynamics: MSF publicized the looting in real-time on social media. The destruction of healthcare infrastructure while civilians were fleeing generated widespread outrage online. MSF's statement that 'repeated government-imposed evacuations leave people exposed and cut off from aid' was widely shared as documenting a systematic pattern of civilian harm.

headline: AFRICOM strikes ISIS-Somalia near Bossaso on March 8, 2026
